Modified files: `crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/__init__.py` (version bump), `crmbuilder-v2/README.md` (v0.4 release-note entry), `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.4-closeout-session-record-drafts.md` (SES-024 section appended), `tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_smoke.py` (slice E follow-up — `EXPECTED_ENTRIES` extended with `"CRM Candidates"` to make the regression gate produce clean signal), `tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_about_dialog.py` (v0.4 closeout test rename + assertion).
after_payload.conversation_reference: Claude Code execution conversation executing `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.4-F-closeout.md` via the `run-v0.4-slice.sh F` launcher. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
after_payload.in_flight_at_end: None — v0.4 build complete. Operator-authored closeout records (SES-017 through SES-024 plus DEC-068 through DEC-074 plus PI-013/014/015 plus status update from "v0.3 complete" to "v0.4 complete") are ready to author per `v0.4-closeout-session-record-drafts.md`.
after_payload.summary: Sixth and final slice of v0.4 build — mechanical closeout. Five deliverables landed in one commit: (1) `__version__` in `crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/__init__.py` bumped from `"0.3.0"` to `"0.4.0"`; (2) v0.4 release-note entry added to `crmbuilder-v2/README.md` directly above the v0.3 entry, matching v0.3's paragraph-plus-bulleted-highlights shape and naming the four new methodology panels, the two new vocab kinds, the Methodology sidebar group, the bidirectional handoff rendering, and the SES-010 next-identifier helper retrofit; (3) regression `uv run pytest tests/crmbuilder_v2/` confirmed green via the slice-E batched-run posture (`access + bootstrap + mcp_server + api` together = 533 passed + 1 skipped, then `ui/` = 554 passed; total 1087 passed + 1 skipped). The monolithic full-suite invocation hit `Fatal Python error: Aborted` mid-flight in `tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_entities_panel.py` from an anyio-portal / FastAPI-TestClient / Qt threading interaction — the same flake slice E hit and recorded as the reason for batched runs. Pre-bump baseline full-suite did complete cleanly with the two pre-existing slice-E test failures noted in judgment call (a) below; (4) integration smoke confirmed Methodology sidebar group renders all four entries (Domains / Entities / Processes / CRM Candidates) by code-inspection plus the regression suite (the live desktop app could not be driven interactively from this Claude Code session; the regression suite plus `_resolve_version()` smoke verifying `0.4.0` cover every behavior the manual smoke would have checked), and About dialog reads `0.4.0`; (5) this SES-024 draft appended to `v0.4-closeout-session-record-drafts.md` between SES-023 and the Section 2 header. PRD §6 acceptance criteria F1–F5 all pass; the cumulative roll-up (A1–A8 plus all 57 per-entity criteria) carries forward from slices A–E. Two judgment calls. (a) The pre-flight baseline test pass detected two pre-existing failures in `tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_smoke.py` (`test_sidebar_entries_constant` and `test_main_window_constructs`) caused by slice E adding `"CRM Candidates"` to `SIDEBAR_ENTRIES` without updating the test's `EXPECTED_ENTRIES` constant. Per the F-closeout prompt's "fix happens in the slice that owns the failing test, not in slice F" rule, the strict reading would have stopped here. Per Doug's no-stop directive for this conversation, the trivial one-line constant update was folded into slice F's commit as a closeout cleanup since the alternative — a separate slice E follow-up commit for a single missed line in a test constant — would pollute history without changing semantics. (b) The post-bump regression then surfaced one further failure in `tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_about_dialog.py::test_version_displays_0_3_0`, which hardcoded the v0.3 closeout's expected version. Renamed to `test_version_displays_0_4_0` with the asserted version updated to `"0.4.0"` — this is the v0.4 closeout's parallel of the v0.3 closeout test, mechanically tied to the version bump deliverable in step 1, so its update belongs to slice F regardless of the strict rule. Both deviations identified explicitly in the commit message and in this session record so the changes are traceable.
after_payload.title: v0.4 slice F — closeout
after_payload.topics_covered: Version bump to 0.4.0, README v0.4 release note, full-suite regression pass, integration smoke verification, Methodology sidebar group end-to-end verification, About dialog version verification, session record draft append, slice E follow-up fix folded into slice F (test_smoke.py EXPECTED_ENTRIES constant updated for CRM Candidates), v0.4 closeout test rename (test_about_dialog.py `test_version_displays_0_3_0` → `test_version_displays_0_4_0`), v0.4 build complete.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Forcing all four specs into the original spec-guide section 6 conventions (rejected — would have required either dropping `process` status entirely or adding a vestigial single-value field; would have required redesigning `crm_candidate` to use soft-delete for legitimate removal). Retroactive governance retrofit as part of v0.4 (rejected — substantial migration; carrying as PI-006).
after_payload.consequences: The four MVS schemas may ship as-is. Spec-guide section 6 amended with v1.1 entry. Future methodology entity types added in v0.5+ inherit the new conventions. Governance entities retain their conventions. PI-006 tracks the retrofit decision for when (and if) v2's governance entities should be aligned to the methodology conventions.
after_payload.context: The methodology-entity-schema-design workstream produced four schema specs across SES-012 through SES-015. Each spec independently passed its per-spec completeness gate. Spec-guide section 7.2 requires a cross-spec consistency check at the head of the v0.4-build-planning conversation before integration. The two cross-spec conventions established mid-workstream (parent-prefix field naming and source-first relationship-kind naming) were not pre-decided in the original spec guide and required reconciliation against the section 6 conventions table.
after_payload.decision: The cross-spec consistency check across `domain.md`, `entity.md`, `process.md`, `crm_candidate.md` is accepted as passing per spec-guide section 7.2, with three documented deviations admitted as well-justified: (a) `process` has no `status` field — `process_classification` carries that role and a single-value status would be empty (DEC-055); (b) `crm_candidate` uses `removed` as a first-class terminal status rather than soft-delete because mid-engagement candidate drops are consultant-driven adjustments not authoring errors (DEC-062); (c) `process` uses the 4-letter `PROC` identifier prefix rather than the 3-letter `PRC` because `PRC` reads as a typo (DEC-056). Spec-guide section 6 is amended to capture the two new cross-spec conventions established by SES-012 and applied across SES-013/014/015 (parent-prefix field naming per DEC-046; source-first `{source}_{verb}_{target}` relationship-kind naming for methodology vocab per DEC-048), with scope explicitly forward-only for methodology entity types (governance entity types retain their pre-workstream conventions until and unless PI-006 retrofit lands).
after_payload.rationale: The three deviations are each justified inline in their respective spec's section 3 by their authoring decisions. `process` having no status is not a missing field but a structural correctness — process classification IS process status. `crm_candidate.removed` is the cleaner semantic for a candidate that was real but is no longer in consideration. `PROC` over `PRC` is readability. The spec-guide section 6 amendment is forward-only because retroactively renaming governance entity fields would be a substantial migration with no current customer value; PI-006 tracks that retrofit decision separately.
after_payload.title: v0.4 cross-spec consistency check accepted with three documented deviations; spec guide section 6 amended for forward-only methodology-entity conventions

after_payload.alternatives_considered: All-in-one (rejected per context). Per-entity-only (rejected per context). Foundation + closeout only with all four entity panels in one slice (rejected — too much UI work in a single slice for clean testability).
after_payload.consequences: Six slice prompts produced and committed. Build sequence A → B → C → D → E → F with linear dependencies (B–E depend on A; F depends on A–E). Each slice has its own commit and acceptance gate.
after_payload.context: v0.4 introduces four new entity types with shared foundation work (vocab additions, CHECK extensions, sidebar group container, retrofit) and panel-specific work per type. Three plausible breakdowns: (a) all-in-one mega-slice (rejected — too risky and untestable mid-flight); (b) per-entity slices only with foundation folded into first entity slice (rejected — foundation work then sits on the critical path of one entity's slice rather than being independently verifiable); (c) hybrid foundation + per-entity + closeout (selected — mirrors v0.3, slice-by-slice testable, foundation independently verifiable).
after_payload.decision: v0.4 is decomposed into six independently-testable slices mirroring v0.3's hybrid pattern: Slice A foundation (vocab, refs CHECK migration, sidebar group container, eight retrofitted next-identifier helpers, spec guide section 6 amendment verification), Slices B/C/D/E one entity panel each in workstream order (Domain, Entity, Process, CRM Candidate), Slice F mechanical closeout (version bump, README, regression pass, smoke).
after_payload.rationale: The hybrid structure is precedented (v0.3 used it for the governance UI build) and produced clean tests. Per-entity panel slices have stable scopes given the four MVS schemas. Foundation as its own slice means slice B starts from a known-good vocab/migration state rather than wrestling with foundation issues during entity-specific work.
after_payload.title: v0.4 slice breakdown — hybrid six-slice structure (foundation + four entity panels + closeout)

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B (create-with-attach: include reference multi-select widgets in the New dialog) — rejected per rationale; documented as a clean v0.5 upgrade if CBM redo surfaces friction.
after_payload.consequences: Slices C and D both implement create-then-attach uniformly. Domain FK on Process is a scalar required field IN the create dialog (record can't submit without it); references are NOT in the create dialog. PI candidate for create-with-attach upgrade noted in PRD risks section.
after_payload.context: v0.3 established the cascading reference creation dialog. v0.4's entity types are the first to ship with outgoing references baked into the spec (entity affiliations, process handoffs). The question is whether the New dialog should also offer reference attachment, or whether attachment is purely a detail-pane operation.
after_payload.decision: Both the Entities panel (Slice C) and the Processes panel (Slice D) implement reference-attachment as a two-stage operation. Stage 1: the New dialog creates the record only, with fields covering scalar attributes only — including required FK fields like `process_domain_identifier`, which are scalars not references. Stage 2: after the record exists, the user attaches references from the detail pane via the existing "Add reference" affordance, which opens v0.3's cascading vocab dialog with the source pre-populated. Option A (create-then-attach) chosen over Option B (create-with-attach: include reference multi-select widgets in the New dialog).
after_payload.rationale: Option A reuses the existing v0.3 affordance verbatim with no new UI work and produces consistent flows across all four panels (Domain and CRM Candidate have no outgoing references; Entity and Process do, but their New dialogs match Domain/CRM Candidate's no-references-in-New shape). Option B would introduce a new multi-select widget pattern and would couple record creation to reference validation, which complicates error surfacing. Option A's cost is more clicks for the consultant; Option B's cost is more UI implementation and one more error-surface to test.
after_payload.title: Coordinated create-then-attach reference-attachment flow for entity affiliations and process handoffs

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Split into Slice A1 (foundation) + Slice A2 (retrofit) — rejected as unnecessary subdivision for a small, coherent change set.
after_payload.consequences: Slice A scope is larger than slices B–E but still testable in one commit. All twelve next-identifier endpoints (4 new methodology + 8 retrofitted governance) ship together with one shared util.
after_payload.context: SES-010 surfaced an asymmetry: v0.3 governance entity types lacked next-identifier helpers, requiring identifier-aware clients to compute prefixes themselves. DEC-043 settled to retrofit the eight types. The retrofit could land as its own slice (Slice A1) or fold into Slice A.
after_payload.decision: The SES-010 identifier-asymmetry resolution per DEC-043 — retrofitting `GET /<entity>/next-identifier` helper endpoints onto the eight existing prefixed-identifier governance entity types — is folded into Slice A foundation rather than split into a separate slice.
after_payload.rationale: The retrofit is small (one new helper util + eight endpoint additions + ~14 tests), shares the foundation surface with the new methodology entity types' helpers (all twelve helpers share the same shared util), and produces no user-facing UI surface. Folding into Slice A keeps slice count at six and gives Slice A a coherent "everything needed before the first entity panel ships" scope.
after_payload.title: Slice A foundation scope includes the SES-010 next-identifier retrofit

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B (status-grouped) — rejected per rationale; reserved as v0.5+ candidate gated on CBM-redo signal.
after_payload.consequences: Slice E master pane sorts by identifier ascending. v0.5+ planning conversation will revisit if CBM redo Phase 1 surfaces visual friction.
after_payload.context: `crm_candidate` has four statuses with three terminal states. In a typical CBM engagement, candidate counts are small (3–5 active candidates initially, possibly 1–2 in `selected` after evaluation, the rest `declined` or `removed`). Two sort options are reasonable: simple identifier-ascending (Option A — terminal states interleave with active by identifier), or status-grouped (Option B — terminal states sink below active).
after_payload.decision: The `crm_candidate` master pane in slice E uses simple identifier-ascending sort as its default (Option A). Status-then-identifier ordering (Option B — `active` first, then `selected`, `declined`, `removed` interleaved with `active` by identifier within each status group) is reserved as a v0.5+ candidate gated on CBM-redo signal.
after_payload.rationale: At small counts, identifier-ascending is fine and matches the master-pane convention used by Domain and Entity panels. Status-grouped becomes more useful once candidate counts grow past ~10 with multiple terminal-state records cluttering the view. v0.4 doesn't have CBM-redo evidence yet that the count will grow that far; Option A is the conservative ship.
after_payload.title: crm_candidate master-pane sort — simple identifier-ascending in v0.4; status-then-identifier reserved for v0.5+

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Including PI-006 in v0.4 (rejected — substantial migration with no current value). Including PI-008 in v0.4 (rejected — independent scope deserving its own planning). Splitting either into a separate workstream-parallel slice (rejected — adds branch complexity without v0.4 benefit).
after_payload.consequences: Both PIs remain in the database tracked but deferred. v0.5+ planning revisits.
after_payload.context: PI-006 and PI-008 were carried into the v0.4-build-planning conversation as candidate inclusions. Both are substantial. PI-006 touches eight existing governance entity tables plus their downstream surfaces. PI-008 introduces a new UI subsystem (folder watcher) with its own architectural questions (which folder, duplicate handling, watcher error surfacing).
after_payload.decision: Both PI-006 (governance-entity retrofit to parent-prefix field-naming convention) and PI-008 (in-app inbox folder watcher for close-out JSON payloads) are confirmed deferred to v0.5+ and explicitly out of v0.4 scope.
after_payload.rationale: v0.4 is already a substantial release scope (four new entity types end-to-end). Including either PI doubles the work without proportional value. PI-006 has no current customer-facing surface (governance entities work fine with their current conventions); the new methodology entity types ship with the new conventions regardless. PI-008 is independent of methodology entity work and warrants its own planning conversation. Both belong in v0.5+ planning if/when prioritized.
after_payload.title: PI-006 (governance-entity parent-prefix retrofit) and PI-008 (in-app inbox folder watcher) both deferred to v0.5+ outside v0.4 scope

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Renumber catalog ingestion's DEC-065/066/067 instead (rejected — those decisions have already been recorded with their numbers; rewriting database history is more disruptive than renumbering the not-yet-recorded v0.4 decisions). Defer v0.4 approval pending some other ID-allocation scheme (rejected — the renumbering is simple and the build prompts are otherwise ready).
after_payload.consequences: v0.4 PRD status transitions from "Draft — pending approval" to "Approved". v0.4 build prompts unblocked. Slice F prompt updated to produce its own session record draft at slice close (to close the gap that per-slice session-record authoring was not previously inlined in slice prompts). PI-013/014/015 enter the database tracked at v0.5+.
after_payload.context: The v0.4-build-planning conversation drafted its DEC numbers anticipating SES-016 and DEC-065/066/067 would be available. Between draft and approval, the catalog ingestion build executed and claimed those IDs. The collision required renumbering before the v0.4 PRD could close out as approved.
after_payload.decision: v0.4 PRD `ui-PRD-v0.4.md` is approved with renumbering deltas applied: the anticipated SES-016 and DEC-065 through DEC-070 numbering shifts to SES-017 and DEC-068 through DEC-073 because the catalog ingestion build (SES-016, executed 05-14-26) consumed those IDs during the gap between v0.4-build-planning conversation and approval. Three new planning items added to PRD §2 Out of Scope: PI-013 Cross-Domain Service representation, PI-014 Catalog FK integration for methodology entities, PI-015 Methodology entity renderers — surfaced by the reconciliation conversation (SES-018) as previously-untracked v0.5+ work. PRD §11 (Decisions to Be Recorded) updated to reflect the renumbering and the additional PIs. Implementation plan and spec guide updated in lockstep. Build prompts B/C/D/E retain inline semantic DEC anchors at their drafted numbers (DEC-067, DEC-069) as documentation references; PRD §11 carries the authoritative renumbering table for any reader needing to resolve them. Slice F prompt and foundation prompt updated with operator-facing references corrected to the renumbered identifiers.
after_payload.rationale: The collision is bookkeeping, not a substantive design issue. Renumbering preserves all decision content while satisfying the database's unique-identifier constraint. The PRD's renumbering note documents the shift transparently for future readers.
after_payload.title: v0.4 PRD approval pass with renumbering deltas after catalog ingestion ID collision

after_payload.description:
Target version: v0.5+.

The original methodology named Cross-Domain Service (Notes, Email, Calendar, Surveys) as a methodology entity type consumed across domains. The evolved methodology has not yet resolved whether Cross-Domain Service remains a distinct entity type, is subsumed into `process` once `process_kind` lands in v0.5+, or is dropped. The v0.4 schema workstream did not address the question and the original methodology-entity-schema-planning-prompt did not flag the gap. Resolution waits on CBM-redo signal: if Phase 1 surfaces work that the evolved methodology treats as cross-domain in nature, evaluate whether `process` with a future `process_kind` field is the right home, or whether a separate `cross_domain_service` entity type is warranted. Decision gate: at the first CBM-redo Phase 1 conversation that touches Notes / Email / Calendar / Surveys content. Surfaced by the 05-14-26 reconciliation conversation (SES-018).
after_payload.title: Cross-Domain Service representation

after_payload.description:
Target version: v0.5+.

The catalog ingestion PRD's section 3.3 sketched a hybrid integration pattern: methodology entities carry an optional `primary_catalog_entity_id` FK plus DEC-006 universal references for weak ties. The four v0.4 methodology schemas (`domain`, `entity`, `process`, `crm_candidate`) do not implement the FK because their MVS thin shape doesn't carry the field-level entity definitions that would need it. When PI-004's `field` entity type lands in v0.5+, the catalog FK question must be settled: (a) integer FK to `catalog_entity.id` vs text FK to `catalog_entity.catalog_id` per the catalog DEC-065 stable-identifier convention; (b) FK on `entity` vs on `field`; (c) how universal-references weak ties interact with the FK semantics. Scope: tied to PI-004 prerequisite; design conversation opens when PI-004's design conversation does, not independently. Surfaced by the 05-14-26 reconciliation conversation (SES-018).
after_payload.title: Catalog FK integration for methodology entities

after_payload.description:
Target version: v0.5+ or later.

DEC-008 prescribes that v2 produces renders, not authored copies — Word documents, deployment YAML, and JSON exports are all generated from the database, not maintained alongside it. v0.4 ships the storage and UI surface for the four MVS methodology entity types but zero renderer work. Three render targets are forecast: (a) .docx generation for Domain Inventory and Phase 1 Prioritized Backbone documents (per evolved-methodology Phase 1 outputs); (b) YAML generation for the v1 deployment engine (current YAML lives in client repositories as hand-authored files); (c) per-entity JSON exports for git-diff via the existing DEC-008 hook (these may already exist mechanically through the storage layer's standard export pattern — verify before scoping). Likely a sizable workstream on its own; opening it before v0.4 ships is premature. A separate planning conversation should follow either v0.4 closeout or first CBM-redo iteration, whichever comes first. Surfaced by the 05-14-26 reconciliation conversation (SES-018).
after_payload.title: Methodology entity renderers

after_payload.description:
Target version: v0.5+.

The live `/references` router does not enforce per-pair `RELATIONSHIP_RULES` because doing so would break v0.3 regression tests that use cross-pair references like `session → decision / decided_in`. Per-pair vocab compliance is currently enforced only at the cascading `ReferenceCreateDialog`, which means the desktop UI is safe but the REST surface is not. If the API is consumed only by the desktop UI (which routes through the dialog), the gap is theoretical. If MCP tools or any external script POSTs directly to `/references` with arbitrary kinds, a non-vocab `kind` could land in the database. Resolution: tighten router-level validation while preserving v0.3 cross-pair governance reference behavior — likely by enforcing per-pair `RELATIONSHIP_RULES` only for pairs where rules exist, leaving pairs with no rules (current v0.3 pattern) unconstrained. Surfaced by SES-021 (v0.4 slice C entities panel build) when implementing criterion 14's "POST /references with (entity, domain) and an unsupported kind returns 422" test, which was satisfied at the schema-rejection level rather than at the router-vocab level.
after_payload.title: Router-level per-pair vocabulary enforcement on `/references`

after_payload.in_flight_at_end: Two Conversation 1's queued in fresh Claude.ai chats: v0.5 Conversation 1 (against v0.5-conversation-1-kickoff.md, settles the ten architectural questions and produces multi-engagement-architecture.md + methodology-schema-specs/engagement.md) and styling Conversation 1 (against styling-conversation-1-kickoff.md, produces styling-design-pass.md). Either can open first; they run independently. After both Conversation 1's close, two Conversation 2's open: v0.5 build planning (produces ui-PRD-v0.5.md + implementation plan + slice prompts) and styling build planning (produces styling PRD + slice prompts plus settles the v0.5-bundle-or-v0.6-separate version question). after_payload.title: v0.5 orientation — engagement management workstream kickoff, PI-001 reopening, paper-test deferral
after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: 'Continued work on V2' against the project default ARCHITECTURE operating mode.

Discovery walk through repository state. Read CLAUDE.md (684 lines, root), the v2 db-export JSON snapshots (status.json v13 'v0.4 complete', sessions.json with 24 entries, decisions.json with 74 entries, planning_items.json with 16 entries, plus references, charter, change_log), the four MVS methodology entity schema specs at methodology-schema-specs/, ui-PRD-v0.4.md, the workstream plans, the conduct framework at PRDs/process/conduct/, the evolved-methodology phase outline, the catalog ingestion artifacts, and the recent paper-test kickoff at methodology-schemas-cbm-paper-test-kickoff.md authored earlier same day.

State reconciliation. User memory was last updated around SES-011 (05-11-26) and was 5 days stale relative to actual state. Between SES-011 and conversation open: schema-design conversations SES-012–015 (domain, entity, process, crm_candidate), catalog ingestion SES-016 (42-entry base entity catalog into v2 database), v0.4-build-planning SES-017, v0.4 PRD reconciliation SES-018, v0.4 slices A–F as SES-019–024, conduct framework integration commits 2f47c20 + a5cb163, and the paper-test kickoff commit 75f2432. Status payload now reads 'phase: v0.4 complete'.

Surfacing of the actual confusion. Six candidate sources of confusion catalogued: memory drift, the unresolved coexistence of the current 13-phase Document Production Process and the evolved 5-phase methodology, three different meanings of 'entity' (methodology entity type / the methodology entity type literally named 'entity' / catalog entity), v1 and v2 not yet connected for the deployment path, PI-001 four-times-deferred styling, and the paper-test authored-but-unrun status. User pointed at the data-co-mingling problem: dogfooded v2.db is reserved for v2-build governance; testing CBM there would mix two unrelated bodies of data.

The DEC-039 multi-tenancy posture (one v2 instance per engagement; separate SQLite, separate API port; finding-not-decision) was rediscovered together with the operational mechanism gap (the code supports env-var-pointed alternate instances via CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH, CRMBUILDER_V2_API_PORT, CRMBUILDER_V2_API_BASE_URL, but no second instance has been created, no naming convention exists, and no runbook exists). The 'no second instance' state is exactly what would have been hit mid-Phase-1 if the paper-test had opened next.

Discovery of v1's existing data model. Read automation/db/master_schema.py (Client master DB with id/name/code/project_folder/crm_platform/deployment_model fields), automation/db/client_schema.py (31-table per-client SQLite schema at {project_folder}/.crmbuilder/{code}.db with Domain/Entity/Field/FieldOption/Relationship/Persona/BusinessObject/Process/ProcessStep/Requirement/Decision/AISession/ChangeLog/Layout*/Instance/DeploymentRun tables), automation/importer/ (25 files, Path B parsers and mappers for master_prd/business_object_discovery/domain_overview/domain_reconciliation/entity_prd/process_definition/crm_selection/crm_deployment/yaml_generation). Side-by-side comparison surfaced that v1 already has nearly every methodology entity type v2's deferred PIs (PI-003 persona, PI-004 field/requirement/manual_config/test_spec, PI-005 process_step) catalog as future work. v2's stated 'database as source of truth' rationale (DEC-001) is the same architectural goal v1 has been incrementally pursuing for two years.

Reframing from the user. The v2 plan was governance-first then incremental migration of v1 functionality onto v2's foundation. Engagement management is just the next migration item — not a deviation from the plan. PI-003/004/005 are similarly migration items, not new inventions.

The consequential decision settled. Path A (build proper Engagement management in v2 as v0.5) vs Path B (Frankenstein bridge reading v1's master.db) was weighed via the eight-element template. Costs are roughly equivalent (both require a registry mechanism, active-engagement context, switcher UI, launcher, tests). Path A produces no bridge code requiring eventual unwinding; Path B does. Path A wins on the consistent-with-plan and no-technical-debt dimensions. Path C (env-var override only, no UI) was named for completeness but not seriously considered.

v0.5 workstream shape proposed and approved. Three conversations: this v0.5-orientation conversation (produces the workstream plans and the paper-test deferral), Conversation 1 (combined multi-engagement architecture + engagement schema design, because architecture and schema are tightly coupled — engagement record field shape depends on routing decisions), Conversation 2 (v0.5 build planning produces PRD + implementation plan + Claude Code slice prompts). Then build slices A–D and closeout.

Ten architectural questions catalogued for Conversation 1 to settle: discovery model (bootstrap DB vs directory scan), per-engagement DB file location, active-engagement state persistence, API server model (multi-tenant vs one-process-per-engagement), identifier scope (global vs per-engagement), migrations across engagements, the dogfood-migration mechanism, per-engagement exports location, MCP server routing, engagement entity field set.

PI-001 disposition resolved. The four-deferral pattern with progressively-elaborate trigger conditions that never fire was named as an anti-pattern. CBM-redo-friction trigger from DEC-042 cannot fire in v0.5's timeframe either (CBM redo waits on v0.5 + paper-test + Phase 1). Two options weighed: defer a fifth time, or reopen as parallel work alongside v0.5. Reopening chosen because the work is independent of v0.5's data/routing layer, scheduling certainty exceeds clean release scoping, and Phase 1 (post-v0.5) lands on a coherent system rather than a Qt-default one. Boundary discipline named: styling owns visual layer (QSS, design tokens, palette, typography, spacing, panel chrome); v0.5 owns data/routing layer (config, ActiveEngagementContext, alembic, API routing, engagement entity). One coupling point: v0.5's engagement panel inherits whatever tokens are current at slice land time; either order ships fine.

Paper-test disposition resolved. The paper-test kickoff at methodology-schemas-cbm-paper-test-kickoff.md predates v0.5 and assumes one v2 instance. Running it now would land CBM domain content in the v2-build dogfood instance. Deferred until v0.5 ships and a CBM engagement is created. A 'Status: Deferred' blockquote header was added to the kickoff document; body content unchanged.

Close-out path chosen. Path 1 (Claude Code prompt at conversation close, mirroring v0.4's slice G precedent at commit 9cbf02d) selected over Path 2 (typing each record into the desktop New Session dialog). Path 1 produces a reviewable artifact; Path 2 was named for completeness.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Path B — Frankenstein bridge to v1's master.db. Rejected because the work cost is roughly equal to Path A and the path leaves bridge code that must be unwound when v2 eventually builds its own engagement type (which DEC-039's deferred PIs implicitly forecast). Path C — env-var override only, no UI, no v0.5 entity work. Rejected because it kicks the operational mechanism problem down the road without building the registry, the UI, or the migration; the env-var mechanism exists in code today and the gap is everything around it. Path A' — same as Path A but treated as a single-sprint focused build. Considered as a fallback if delay matters more than scope discipline; not the chosen posture because v0.5 is naturally bounded at one entity type plus routing infrastructure and a single-sprint frame doesn't materially compress that.
after_payload.consequences: v0.5 inserts between v0.4 ship and any CBM activity. The paper-test conversation (DEC-077) and CBM redo Phase 1 both wait on v0.5 shipping plus a CBM engagement being created in v2. The v2.db dogfood instance migrates into the new engagement model — the 24 sessions, 74 decisions, 16 PIs, 66 references, 222 change_log entries, 13 status versions, 2 charter versions, and 42 catalog entities all survive the migration into a CRMBUILDER engagement record. Identifier sequences become per-engagement: CBM's first session is SES-001 within the CBM engagement, not SES-025+ in a global sequence. v0.5 does not pick up the deferred methodology PIs (PI-003 persona, PI-004 field/requirement/manual_config/test_spec, PI-005 process_step, PI-013 Cross-Domain Service, PI-014 catalog FK, PI-015 renderers, PI-016 router-vocab) — those remain v0.6+ candidates. The v2 charter's incremental-migration framing is reinforced rather than abandoned; engagement management is the next migration item from v1 in a planned sequence.
after_payload.context: v0.4 shipped four MVS methodology entity types (domain, entity, process, crm_candidate) into the v2 database, with the stated rationale of enabling CBM redo to use v2 as its system of record. v0.4 closeout exposed that those tables live in the same SQLite file (crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db) that hosts the v2-build's own governance (24 sessions, 74 decisions, 16 PIs, etc.). Running CBM Phase 1 against the dogfood instance would co-mingle two unrelated bodies of data in one file with one identifier sequence. DEC-039's working answer to the multi-tenancy question — 'one v2 instance per engagement, separate SQLite, separate API port' — was framed as a finding rather than a designed feature; the operational mechanism (env-var-pointed alternate DB paths) existed in code but no second instance had been created, no naming convention existed, no UI for switching existed, no runbook existed. The v0.5-orientation conversation also surfaced that v1 already has multi-client management: automation/db/master_schema.py defines a Client master table; automation/ui/clients_tab.py ships a Clients tab; automation/db/client_schema.py defines a 31-table per-client methodology schema including Domain/Entity/Field/Persona/Process/ProcessStep/Requirement/Decision/AISession. The v2 charter (DEC-001 and surrounding) frames v2 as the next major iteration of CRMBuilder with v1 functionality migrating onto v2's foundation over time; engagement management is the natural next migration item.
after_payload.decision: **v0.5 builds engagement management properly in v2 as the next migration item from v1.** v0.5 adds one new methodology entity type, `engagement` (or final name confirmed in Conversation 1), structured under MVS-thin scope per the v0.4 pattern: identifier + code (constrained to match v1's Client.code shape so the two systems can co-reference engagements by the same code) + name + purpose + status + audit columns. v0.5 also adds the supporting multi-engagement routing infrastructure: per-engagement SQLite databases, an active-engagement state mechanism, a bootstrap/discovery mechanism for enumerating engagements, an engagement-switcher mechanism in the desktop app, migration handling across engagements, and migration of the existing v2.db dogfood instance into the new engagement model. The workstream is structured as three conversations (this v0.5-orientation conversation; Conversation 1 combining multi-engagement architecture with engagement schema design; Conversation 2 producing v0.5 PRD and implementation plan); strawman four build slices follow. See PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-engagement-management-workstream-plan.md for the full plan.
after_payload.rationale: Equal cost weighs the bridge-to-v1 option down. The Frankenstein path (Path B in the conversation) was named and weighed against building proper engagement management in v2 (Path A). Both paths require comparable work: a registry mechanism, an ActiveEngagementContext analog, a switcher UI or env-var workflow, a launcher, schema-compat handling, tests. The work cost is roughly equivalent. Path A produces no bridge code requiring eventual unwinding; Path B produces bridge code with a known sunset date (when v2 eventually builds its own engagement type). With cost equal, the path without technical debt wins. The migration framing also matters: every deferred v2 PI (PI-003 persona, PI-004 field/requirement/manual_config/test_spec, PI-005 process_step) is implicitly a migration item from v1's per-client schema. Engagement management is in the same category. Building it the v2 way once, at v0.5, is consistent with the broader plan; bridging now and reimplementing later is not.
after_payload.title: v0.5 release scope: add engagement management and multi-instance routing as v2-native capability, not a bridge to v1's Client master DB

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Fifth deferral with the same CBM-redo-friction trigger. Rejected — the trigger has not fired in four cycles and cannot fire in v0.5's timeframe; the deferral is silent abandonment. Fifth deferral with a different trigger (e.g., post-Phase-1 review). Rejected for the same reason in different form — trigger conditions that cannot fire in the planning horizon don't constitute real deferral. v0.5-bundled — ship styling inside v0.5 rather than parallel. Rejected because engagement management and styling are independent capabilities that share no functional dependency; bundling mixes unrelated change in the README and complicates release notes. Sequential v0.5-then-styling — wait for v0.5 to ship before opening styling. Rejected because the work is independent enough that sequencing adds no clarity and parallel execution shortens the gap before CBM Phase 1 lands on a styled system. Retire PI-001 entirely — drop the styling work. Rejected — the work is real and the system needs visual coherence for Phase 1.
after_payload.consequences: PI-001's open status persists but its meaning changes from 'deferred work waiting for a trigger' to 'active parallel workstream with its own plan.' Two Conversation 1's queue in fresh chats after this conversation closes — v0.5's and styling's — and can open in any order. The styling workstream has its own kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-conversation-1-kickoff.md and its own workstream plan at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-workstream-plan.md. Slice coordination at the engagement-panel coupling point is whichever-lands-first wins; the other rebases. Version-bundling decision deferred to the styling workstream's Conversation 2. PI-001's description is amended via PATCH to reflect reopening (see PI-001 in this apply).
after_payload.context: PI-001 was created in DEC-024 at v0.1 close on the principle that v2's early releases should ship structural capability first and visual polish later. Deferred three more times: DEC-026 at v0.2 close, DEC-037 at v0.3 close, DEC-042 at v0.4 close. DEC-042 added an explicit trigger mechanism: PI-001 would open ahead of any other v0.5 candidate if CBM-redo Phase 1 — running against the four methodology panels v0.4 delivered — surfaced visual friction on any of those panels. The v0.5-orientation conversation surfaced that this trigger cannot fire in v0.5's timeframe: CBM redo Phase 1 waits on v0.5 (DEC-075) plus the paper-test (DEC-077). The four-deferral pattern with progressively-elaborate trigger conditions that never fire is an anti-pattern; PI-001 has effectively become a placeholder rather than a real signal. Two paths: defer a fifth time with the same trigger that has never worked, or reopen as parallel work.
after_payload.decision: **Reopen PI-001 as a parallel workstream alongside v0.5, executing independently of v0.5 build with its own kickoff, design pass, build planning, and slices.** Two-conversation structure mirroring v0.5's shape but smaller: Conversation 1 produces the design pass (palette, typography scale, spacing scale, radius/border/elevation tokens, density target, visual decisions for major component classes — output at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-design-pass.md); Conversation 2 produces the styling PRD and slice build prompts. Strawman five build slices follow: foundation (tokens module, base widget refactors, sidebar, About dialog), governance panel retrofit, methodology panel retrofit, dialog and form-control polish, closeout. PI-001's status changes from 'Open (deferred 4×)' to 'Open (active — parallel workstream)'. The version-bundling question (ship as v0.5 vs. ship as v0.6) is left for the styling workstream's Conversation 2 to settle. Boundary discipline between the two workstreams: styling owns the visual layer (QSS, design tokens, palette/typography/spacing, panel chrome, sidebar visuals, About dialog, hover/focus/disabled states); v0.5 owns the data/routing layer (config.py, ActiveEngagementContext, alembic-per-engagement, API server routing, engagement entity type, engagement-switcher mechanism). One real coupling point: v0.5's new engagement panel (slice B in the strawman) inherits whatever design tokens have shipped at the time the slice lands — either order ships fine. See PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-workstream-plan.md for the full plan.
after_payload.rationale: Three things changed since the four-deferral pattern was established. First, the no-production-use condition is now structural rather than incidental — v2's only user is the v2 build itself, whose friction is largely with structural decisions (schema, routing, identifiers) rather than visual treatment. Even when CBM Phase 1 starts (post-v0.5), the data shape is thin enough that visual friction will be muted. Second, the system is no longer small — v0.4 ships sixteen panels across two sidebar groups, five dialogs, two scope-level concerns; designing a visual language now is comprehensive design, not a quick polish. Third, the deferral mechanism has become an anti-pattern — each deferral added another trigger condition; none have ever fired; the open PI has become a placeholder. Either the work is real and should be done, or PI-001 should be retired. The conversation concluded the former. Parallel execution rather than v0.5-then-styling-sequential is chosen because the work is independent of v0.5's data/routing layer (see boundary discipline), scheduling certainty exceeds clean release scoping at this point, and CBM Phase 1 (when it begins post-v0.5) benefits from landing on a coherent system rather than a Qt-default one.
after_payload.title: PI-001 (styling design pass) reopens as parallel workstream alongside v0.5, replacing fifth deferral

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Run the paper-test against the dogfood instance, accept co-mingling. Rejected — co-mingling is exactly what v0.5 prevents; defeating v0.5's purpose in advance defeats the rationale for the v0.5 detour. Run the paper-test against a manually-spun-up second v2 instance via env-var override before v0.5 ships. Rejected — the env-var mechanism exists but no second instance has been created, no naming convention exists, and the operational debt would still be incurred without v0.5's UI / registry / switching mechanism. Move the kickoff document to a 'deferred/' subfolder. Rejected as premature; a status header is sufficient and avoids breaking any pre-existing references to the path. Edit the kickoff body to anticipate v0.5. Rejected — anticipating v0.5 before v0.5 ships invents specifics the actual Conversation 1's might revise. Light run-time editing at the start of the paper-test conversation is the cleaner approach.
after_payload.consequences: The paper-test conversation does not run next. It runs after v0.5 ships and a CBM engagement record is created. The kickoff document is annotated with a Status: Deferred header that names the predecessor work; the document body remains identical. The light run-time editing required to reflect that v0.5 has shipped happens in the paper-test's actual opening conversation, not pre-emptively.
after_payload.context: The paper-test conversation kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-schemas-cbm-paper-test-kickoff.md was authored 05-15-26 11:00 (commit 75f2432) and was framed as the next-up conversation after v0.4 closeout. The kickoff describes a structured validation conversation reading existing CBM domain content against the four v0.4 MVS schemas (domain, entity, process, crm_candidate) and producing two deliverables: a findings report and a single decision — 'ship CBM redo Phase 1 on v0.4 as-is' or 'amend N schemas first.' The kickoff predates v0.5 and assumes there is one v2 instance available to test against. DEC-075 inserts v0.5 between v0.4 ship and any CBM activity; running the paper-test now would land CBM domain content in the v2-build dogfood instance.
after_payload.decision: **Defer the paper-test conversation until v0.5 ships and a CBM engagement record is created.** A 'Status: Deferred' blockquote header is added at the top of methodology-schemas-cbm-paper-test-kickoff.md naming the predecessor work (v0.5 ships; CBM engagement created in v2 with its own SQLite database; light run-time editing to reflect those changes). The kickoff body is unchanged because the paper-test's scope and method are unaffected — only the timing changes. The paper-test runs against the freshly-created CBM engagement, which is empty until CBM Phase 1 begins.
after_payload.rationale: Running the paper-test against the dogfood instance would co-mingle CBM domain content with the v2-build's own governance content in one SQLite file with one identifier sequence — exactly the data-co-mingling problem v0.5 exists to solve (DEC-075). Deferring is the correct action; the alternative is doing the work twice (once now in the dogfood, again post-v0.5 in the proper CBM engagement) or accepting permanent co-mingling. The deferral is timing-only, not scope-or-method, so no kickoff body changes are required; a header note suffices.
after_payload.title: Paper-test conversation deferred until v0.5 ships and a CBM engagement record is created

after_payload.description: Full styling design pass per DEC-024. Originally deferred four times (DEC-024 → DEC-026 → DEC-037 → DEC-042) on the CBM-redo-friction trigger principle. **Reopened as a parallel workstream by DEC-076 (05-16-26)** because the trigger condition (CBM-redo Phase 1 surfacing visual friction on the four v0.4 methodology panels) cannot fire in v0.5's timeframe — CBM redo waits on v0.5 (DEC-075) plus the paper-test (DEC-077) plus Phase 1 itself, and the four-deferral pattern with progressively-elaborate trigger conditions that never fire has become an anti-pattern. The pass establishes a coherent visual language for the v2 desktop application: design tokens (palette with semantic naming, typography scale, spacing scale, radius/border/elevation tokens, density target), visual decisions for major component classes (panels, sidebar, buttons, form controls, dialogs, tables), and implementation across all governance and methodology panels. **Workstream structure (per `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-workstream-plan.md`):** two conversations — Conversation 1 produces the design pass document at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-design-pass.md`; Conversation 2 produces the styling PRD, implementation plan, and slice build prompts. Strawman five build slices follow (foundation; governance panel retrofit; methodology panel retrofit; dialog and form-control polish; closeout). **Boundary discipline with v0.5 (per DEC-076):** styling owns the visual layer (QSS, design tokens, panel chrome, sidebar visuals, About dialog, hover/focus/disabled states); v0.5 owns the data/routing layer (config, ActiveEngagementContext, alembic, API routing, engagement entity). One coupling point: v0.5's engagement panel inherits whatever tokens are current at slice land time; either order ships fine. **Version-bundling question** (v0.5 vs. v0.6) deferred to the styling workstream's Conversation 2.
after_payload.title: Full styling design pass per DEC-024

before_payload.description: Full styling design pass per DEC-024, deferred FOUR TIMES: v0.1→v0.2 (DEC-024), v0.2→v0.3 (DEC-026), v0.3→future (DEC-037), v0.4→v0.5+ with CBM-redo-friction trigger (DEC-042). The pass establishes a coherent visual language for the v2 desktop application: typography hierarchy, accent colors beyond the navy stub (#1F3864), error/warning/info states, button hierarchy, dialog framing, table row spacing, and accessibility considerations. **Trigger mechanism (per DEC-042):** if CBM redo Phase 1 — running against the four new methodology entity panels delivered in v0.4 (domain, entity, process, crm_candidate) — surfaces visual friction on any of those panels, PI-001 gets pulled to v0.5 ahead of any other v0.5 candidate, regardless of v0.5 planning's other priorities. 'Visual friction' is intentionally fuzzy: Doug's judgment as the consultant running the redo determines whether something he sees while working bugs him enough to count. If CBM redo runs smoothly visually, PI-001 may continue to defer — that's evidence too.
before_payload.title: Full styling design pass per DEC-024

Plus the close-out payload PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_026.json (this payload) and the apply prompt PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-026.md. All four artifacts committed to origin/main in one commit by Claude per the in-sandbox close-out convention.

Combined multi-engagement architecture design with engagement methodology-entity schema design because the two were tightly coupled (engagement record field shape depends on routing decisions). after_payload.title: v0.5 Conversation 1 — multi-engagement architecture and engagement schema
after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-conversation-1-kickoff.md against the project default ARCHITECTURE operating mode.

Pre-flight. Read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md, PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-engagement-management-workstream-plan.md (the ten architectural questions agenda), methodology-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, methodology-schema-specs/domain.md end-to-end plus the §3 heading structure of entity.md, process.md, and crm_candidate.md (confirming template uniformity and noting process's no-status-field deviation and crm_candidate's removed-status deviation), the v0.4 PRD §2/§6/§7-9, the v2 runtime config.py (existing env-var override mechanism), v1's automation/db/master_schema.py (Client.code regex constraint and master-DB pattern precedent), v1's automation/ui/active_client_context.py (in-memory + signal + dual-persistence pattern), DEC-039 (the original multi-tenancy finding), and the SES-025 close-out payload (DEC-075/076/077). Verified SES-025 close-out had been applied: sessions=25, decisions=77, three decided_in refs to SES-025 present, PI-001 status still Open with description amended. git status clean; git pull --rebase clean.

Framing note before Q1. DEC-039 is the prior context being operationalised, not superseded. The weak priors in the kickoff all match DEC-039's framing exactly. Two precedents banked for later questions: v1's database_path column was deprecated in favor of convention (real-world supporting evidence for Q2's convention-based-paths posture); v1's active-state persistence is actually hybrid (Client.last_opened_at on master row plus last_selected_client_id in preferences), which informed Q3's framing.

Q1 — Engagement discovery model. Bootstrap meta DB vs directory scan. Option A approved: bootstrap meta DB at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db with an engagements registry table. Separates routing metadata from methodology content; mirrors v1's master-DB pattern; supports operations on inactive engagements. Sub-question resolved later in the conversation: the engagement record IS the meta DB row (not a pointer to records inside per-engagement DBs).

Q2 — Per-engagement DB file location. Engine-repo conventional vs split vs convention-with-override. Option A approved: engine repo conventional paths at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/{engagement_code}.db, gitignored. No path field on engagement record. v1's split is a real precedent but adopting it now adds coupling to client-repo structure that v0.5's known use cases don't require.

Q7 — Dogfood migration. (Taken next because Q1 + Q2 settled enough foundation to make Q7's options concrete.) One-shot explicit migration vs permanent dual-path vs configurable path. Option A approved: one-shot explicit migration with backup-first, verify-row-counts, then delete-original discipline. Idempotent on rerun. Three install scenarios handled: existing v2 install (migrate), fresh install (no-op, meta DB empty, user creates engagements explicitly), rerun after successful migration (exit cleanly). The 24 sessions / 74 decisions / 16 PIs / 66 references / 222 change_log entries / 42 catalog entries / 13 status versions / 2 charter versions all survive.

Q3 — Active-engagement state persistence. Two distinct facts framed: live state (singleton at v2-install level) and ordering (per-engagement). Option A approved for live state: JSON file at crmbuilder-v2/data/current_engagement.json holding {engagement_identifier, engagement_code, set_at} plus in-memory ActiveEngagementContext QObject mirroring v1's pattern. Ordering: engagements.last_opened_at column on the meta DB. The hybrid mirrors v1's actual pattern.

Q4 — API server model. One-process-per-engagement vs multi-tenant vs hot-swap admin endpoint. Option A approved for v0.5 (one process per engagement, kill-and-relaunch on switch) with the user-added direction that Option B (multi-tenant) be committed as a follow-on migration. Migration timing clarified through a brief back-and-forth: not 'v0.6 specifically' or 'highest-priority v0.6+ candidate' but 'when v2 transitions from prototype to production.' This trigger is a planned transition rather than a friction signal (avoiding the PI-001 anti-pattern). PI-017 authored to track the migration work.

Q9 — MCP server routing. Forced by Q4. Decided-and-announced: MCP server lifecycle mirrors API — one MCP subprocess per engagement, killed and relaunched on switch. Same constraints, same migration path to multi-tenant at prototype-to-production transition.

Q5 — Identifier scope. Decided-and-announced: per-engagement scope for governance and methodology identifiers (SES, DEC, PI, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT, PROC, CRMC) — happens automatically because v0.4 next-identifier helpers query the active DB connection. Engagement identifiers (ENG) scope to the meta DB — one sequence per v2 install.

Q6 — Migrations across engagements. Lazy at engagement-open vs eager at v2 launch vs explicit only. Option A approved: lazy at engagement-open, mirroring v1's run_client_migrations pattern. Defers work to when needed; failure modes scoped to one engagement at a time; never-opened engagements pay no cost. Meta DB has its own Alembic chain that applies at desktop launch.

Q8 — Per-engagement exports. Split (dogfood in engine repo, client in client repo) via engagement_export_dir field vs all in engine repo vs manual command only. Option A approved: nullable engagement_export_dir absolute-path column on the meta DB; dogfood's value set by the v0.5 migration to the computed engine-repo path; client engagement values set at creation time via the New-Engagement dialog. Null disables auto-export. Q1 sub-question resolved here as a downstream implication: engagement record lives in the meta DB row.

Q10b — Engagement status lifecycle. active/paused/archived with free transitions vs domain pattern (candidate/confirmed/deferred) vs two-state (active/archived). Option A approved: active/paused/archived with free transitions, default starter active. Engagement records are direct creations (not propose-verified by a client), so domain's one-way gate doesn't fit. Three states capture real operational lifecycle without ceremony; free transitions match reality (engagements can resume from any state). Soft-delete independent of status.

Q10 — Engagement entity field set (strawman ratification). Ten fields approved: engagement_identifier (ENG-NNN, server-assigned), engagement_code (regex ^[A-Z][A-Z0-9]{1,9}$ mirroring v1's Client.code constraint exactly, case-insensitive unique), engagement_name (required, case-insensitive unique), engagement_purpose (required, plain text 1-2 sentences), engagement_status (enum active/paused/archived, default active, free transitions), engagement_last_opened_at (nullable, for ordering), engagement_export_dir (nullable absolute path, validated when set), engagement_created_at / engagement_updated_at / engagement_deleted_at (inherited base behavior). All prefixed engagement_ per DEC-046. Three deliberate omissions from the domain pattern: no engagement_description (purpose suffices), no engagement_notes (engagement is routing metadata not methodology content; scratchpad notes belong in the per-engagement DB), no engagement_db_path (Q2 chose convention). No relationships in v0.5 (engagement stands alone in the meta DB; per-engagement DB entities don't reference engagement records because engagement is implicit context for everything in the engagement's own DB). Identifier prefix ENG (3 letters, no collision with existing prefixes). Standard endpoint set served from the meta DB (not from the active engagement's DB). No activate endpoint — activation is desktop-side orchestration (kill API → write current_engagement.json → PATCH last_opened_at → launch new API → emit signal). UI deviation justified: engagement is structurally different from the four v0.4 methodology entity types (v2-install-level routing metadata, not domain-scope content), so it doesn't fit the Methodology sidebar group; two needed affordances (switching UI and management UI); exact visual shape deferred to Conversation 2 in coordination with the styling workstream. Dogfood engagement record (CRMBUILDER) field values defined for migration time.

Architectural detail surfaced and recorded. The v0.5 API server connects to two SQLite databases simultaneously: the meta DB (for /engagements/* endpoints) and the active engagement's DB (for everything else). Recorded in multi-engagement-architecture.md §3.10 and engagement.md §3.5.1.

Activation sequence specified. 12-step desktop-side orchestration documented in multi-engagement-architecture.md §4: reachability check, pre-flight Alembic migration, kill API+MCP subprocesses, write current_engagement.json, PATCH engagement_last_opened_at, update in-memory context, launch new API+MCP subprocesses, emit signal. Total elapsed time: a few seconds for a typical switch.

Deliverables produced and committed. Two documents drafted in-conversation: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/multi-engagement-architecture.md (system-level architecture document) and PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-schema-specs/engagement.md (engagement schema spec conforming to methodology-entity-schema-spec-guide.md). Close-out payload at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_026.json. Apply prompt at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-026.md.

Close-out path chosen. Path 1 (Claude Code apply prompt, mirroring the SES-025 precedent) — the apply prompt POSTs the standard payload via apply_close_out.py.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B — directory scan of crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/*.db with engagement metadata stored inside each engagement's own DB. Rejected because populating the picker requires opening every DB, and engagement metadata (status, last_opened_at, purpose, export_dir) is routing-level concern that doesn't naturally belong inside per-engagement methodology content. Renaming / soft-deleting / archiving operations on inactive engagements would either require opening each DB or living with a degraded picker. Option C (hybrid: scan the filesystem AND maintain a meta DB cache) was considered briefly and rejected as needlessly complex — the meta DB alone is sufficient.
after_payload.consequences: v0.5 ships a meta DB at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db (gitignored) with its own Alembic chain. The dogfood migration (DEC-084) creates the meta DB and inserts the CRMBUILDER engagement row. The engagement record lives as a row in the meta DB; the engagement entity schema (DEC-086) follows this placement. The meta DB drift mode is handled by reachability checks; if a per-engagement DB file is missing at activation time, activation fails with a UX message and the user is prompted via the picker. The meta DB's own migrations apply at desktop launch (failure to migrate is a hard-fail UX). Future enhancement (v0.6+, candidate): a small reconciliation tool that detects out-of-band file moves and offers to update the meta DB's engagement records accordingly; not in v0.5 scope.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's first architectural question (Q1 in the workstream plan §6). v2 needs a mechanism to know what engagements exist on this machine at startup, populate the picker UI, and decide which engagement is active. Two viable options framed: (A) a small bootstrap meta SQLite file at a fixed path with an engagements registry table; (B) directory scan of per-engagement DB files in a known directory. Coupled to the engagement record's location (does it live in the meta DB or inside each per-engagement DB?), to the meaning of operations on inactive engagements, and to drift-recovery posture.
after_payload.decision: Bootstrap meta DB at the fixed path crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db. The meta DB has its own Alembic migration chain (separate from per-engagement chains) and is gitignored. Creating an engagement performs two writes: INSERT into the meta DB's engagements table and create the corresponding per-engagement DB file. Discovery is a SELECT against the meta DB. Engagement endpoints (GET /engagements, POST /engagements, etc.) read and write the meta DB exclusively. The engagement record IS the meta DB row (not a lightweight pointer to records inside each per-engagement DB) — this sub-question is resolved here as well, as a consequence of the meta DB being the canonical home for engagement routing metadata.
after_payload.rationale: Separates routing metadata ('what engagements exist on this machine') from methodology content ('what's inside an engagement'). Mirrors v1's master-DB pattern (automation/db/master_schema.py), which has held up across two years of v1 evolution. Carries metadata (status, last-opened-at, purpose, export_dir) outside any single engagement's DB so engagement records can be queried, renamed, or archived without opening each per-engagement DB. The 'engagement record inside its own per-engagement DB' alternative is self-referential and complicates the discovery semantics; the meta DB framing is cleaner. The additional SQLite file is a real cost but small; the drift mode (per-engagement DB file moved or deleted out-of-band) is handled by a reachability check at activation time, mirroring v1's automation/core/client_reachability.py pattern.
after_payload.title: Engagement discovery model: bootstrap meta DB at fixed path with engagements registry table

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B — split (engine DB in engine repo, client DBs in client repos via required engagement_db_path field). Rejected because it requires adding a path field to the engagement record (per Q10) and couples v0.5 to client-repo structure conventions that v0.5's use cases don't actually require. The portability win (client repo cloned to new machine 'comes with' engagement state) is real but theoretical for v0.5's known use cases; deferring it is fine. Option C — convention with nullable override field. Rejected as a compromise that defers a real decision: the field either exists or it doesn't; 'nullable optional override' usually evolves into 'we should have made it explicit one way or the other.' Q2 commits to no field in v0.5; v0.6+ migration adds the field if and when the use case justifies it.
after_payload.consequences: .gitignore gains crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/. Engagement record schema (DEC-086) does not include engagement_db_path. The dogfood migration (DEC-084) moves crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db to crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db. When (if) the engagement-DB-travels-with-client-repo use case surfaces, a v0.6+ migration adds nullable engagement_db_path column to the meta DB engagements table; existing rows stay null and continue using convention; new rows or edited rows may set the override. Acknowledged asymmetry with Q8 (exports do split between engine and client repos via a path field) is defensible because DB files are gitignored data while exports are git-tracked documents — different concerns, different homes.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q2. Each engagement's SQLite file needs a location on disk. Three viable options: (A) all per-engagement DB files in the engine repo at a conventional path, (B) split — engine DB in engine repo, client engagement DBs in client repos at {project_folder}/.crmbuilder-v2/{code}.db (mirroring v1's split), (C) convention with optional override field on the engagement record. Coupled to Q8 (exports location) and to whether the engagement record carries a path field. v1 has been doing the split (Option B's equivalent) for two years and it works; v1 originally had a database_path column on Client but deprecated it in favor of convention {project_folder}/.crmbuilder/{code}.db — real-world supporting evidence that convention wins over explicit field at this granularity.
after_payload.decision: All per-engagement DB files live at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/{engagement_code}.db in the engine repo, gitignored. The engagement record carries no path field in v0.5. Dogfood DB after migration is crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db; CBM's will be crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CBM.db. Filename derived from engagement_code (uppercase per the engagement_code constraint).
after_payload.rationale: Minimum viable for v0.5; handles both dogfood and CBM cleanly. v1's split is a real precedent worth respecting, but adopting it now adds an engagement-record path field plus a coupling to client-repo structure that v0.5's known use cases (Doug on one laptop, dogfood + CBM active) don't justify. Future migration to v1-style split is small (add nullable engagement_db_path column; null means convention, set means override), so deferring the choice doesn't block it. v1's own deprecation of its database_path column in favor of convention is real-world supporting evidence that the path-field flexibility was not actually used at this granularity.
after_payload.title: Per-engagement DB file location: engine repo with conventional paths derived from engagement_code

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B — singleton row in the meta DB (a one-row active_engagement table or an is_current boolean column on engagements). Rejected because external clients (curl, MCP tools, diagnostic scripts) cannot peek without an API call or SQL helper, and the file/DB drift risk is low in practice because the desktop is the only writer to both. Option C — environment variable only, no persistence. Rejected because it loses the 'open the app, last engagement is loaded' UX without producing meaningful simplification. The hybrid framing (split the two facts) was the framing change that resolved the conversation; once the two facts were named, the file-for-live + column-for-ordering answer was uncontroversial.
after_payload.consequences: crmbuilder-v2/data/current_engagement.json added to .gitignore. engagement_last_opened_at column added to the meta DB engagements table (DATETIME, nullable). Activation sequence in multi-engagement-architecture.md §4 includes both writes (file and column). Drift recovery on app launch: if current_engagement.json references an engagement that doesn't exist in the meta DB or whose DB file is missing, the context starts empty and the picker UX prompts for selection (no silent fallback). The in-memory ActiveEngagementContext follows v1's ActiveClientContext pattern (QObject, Qt signal, reachability check on activation, signal emission). External scripts can cat the file to know which engagement is currently active.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q3. v2 needs a mechanism for the desktop, API subprocess, MCP subprocess, and external scripts to know which engagement is currently active, plus a mechanism for the picker to order engagements by recency. Conversation surfaced that two distinct facts are being persisted: live state ('what is the engine pointing at right now', a singleton at the v2-install level, used by API/MCP at startup, by external scripts that want to know who they're talking to, and by the desktop to restore last-active on launch) and ordering ('when was each engagement last opened', per-engagement metadata, used by the picker). Splitting them clarified the framing — the original Q3 framing conflated the two. v1's active-state persistence is itself a hybrid (Client.last_opened_at on master row plus last_selected_client_id in preferences).
after_payload.decision: Two-channel persistence with each channel canonical for one fact. (1) Live state: a small JSON file at crmbuilder-v2/data/current_engagement.json holds {engagement_identifier, engagement_code, set_at}, atomically written by the desktop on engagement activation. Plus an in-memory ActiveEngagementContext QObject with an active_engagement_changed(object) Qt signal, mirroring v1's ActiveClientContext shape. The desktop reads the file on launch to restore the last-active engagement. (2) Ordering: a last_opened_at DATETIME column on the meta DB engagements table (specifically engagement_last_opened_at per the parent-prefix field-naming convention), updated by the desktop at activation. Used by the picker to surface recent engagements first.
after_payload.rationale: Mirrors v1's hybrid pattern (Client.last_opened_at on master row plus last_selected_client_id in preferences) which has held up across two years of v1 evolution. The JSON file affords external peek/curl access without an extra API endpoint or SQL helper. The meta-DB column affords queryable ordering for the picker without the API needing to read the JSON file. Separation of concerns matches the underlying reality: ordering is per-engagement metadata; live pointer is a singleton at the v2-install level. The original Q3 framing's three options (file on disk, column in meta DB, env var only) all conflated the two facts; splitting them clarifies that the question is really about the live state, and the meta-DB column is useful regardless.
after_payload.title: Active-engagement state persistence: JSON file for live state plus engagements.last_opened_at column for ordering plus in-memory ActiveEngagementContext

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B for v0.5 — multi-tenant from the start. Rejected for v0.5 because it requires refactoring every endpoint to accept engagement context (header or path-prefix), refactoring the access-layer connection model, refactoring tests, and reshaping the MCP server — substantial scope addition that v0.5's stated scope did not include. Option D — hot-swap admin endpoint (single API process with POST /admin/switch-engagement). Rejected because it introduces mutable cross-request state that complicates testing and creates mid-request switch race risk; convenience of fast switching isn't worth the testability cost. Option C — dynamic ports per engagement (multiple subprocesses serving simultaneously on different ports). Rejected because v0.5's use case is one engagement active at a time; the port-management complexity isn't justified. For the migration trigger: 'v0.6 specifically' was named but rejected because forcing CBM Phase 1 to wait for the migration is unnecessary scope tightening; 'highest-priority v0.6+ candidate before features' was named but rejected because it under-specifies the trigger; the prototype-to-production framing is what Doug clarified and what fits the v2 charter narrative.
after_payload.consequences: v0.5 build (Conversation 2 + Claude Code slices) implements the kill-relaunch dance in the desktop's switch path (Slice C in the strawman). The API server itself requires no endpoint refactor for v0.5 — only the addition of meta-DB connection handling for /engagements/* endpoints (per DEC-078, the engagement endpoints serve from the meta DB while other endpoints serve from the active engagement's DB — this two-database API server is documented in multi-engagement-architecture.md §3.10). The MCP server has the same lifecycle. UX cost: a few seconds of API unavailability per switch with a 'Switching engagement...' indicator in the desktop. External clients during the switch see connection-refused or timeout. PI-017 is authored to track the multi-tenant migration with the prototype-to-production trigger. CBM Phase 1 can run on v0.5 (one-process-per-engagement) without waiting for PI-017.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q4 and Q9 (Q9 forced by Q4). The API server today (v0.4) is a subprocess spawned by the desktop at startup, reading CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH from env at startup and serving one DB on port 8765. v0.5 needs to support multiple engagements. Two main viable options: (A) one API subprocess per engagement, bound at spawn, killed and relaunched on engagement switch; (B) multi-tenant single process serving all engagements via header or path-prefix routing. Option D (hot-swap admin endpoint) was named but rejected for mutable-cross-request-state reasons. Q9 (MCP) follows whichever Q4 settles. User directed: approve A for v0.5 but add a decision committing to B. Migration timing was clarified through a brief back-and-forth: not 'v0.6 specifically' or 'highest-priority v0.6+ candidate' but 'when v2 transitions from prototype to production.'
after_payload.decision: v0.5 ships one API subprocess per engagement (Option A): the API subprocess is spawned by the desktop launcher with CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH set to the active engagement's DB path, serves on port 8765 for that engagement's lifetime, and is killed and relaunched on engagement switch (the desktop's kill-relaunch dance). The API server's existing endpoint code is unchanged from v0.4 — no concept of engagement, no request-routing layer. The MCP server has the same lifecycle (one MCP subprocess per engagement, mirroring the API). Committed for the longer term: multi-tenant single-process server (Option B) is the target architecture. The migration from one-process-per-engagement to multi-tenant is committed; it lands as part of the production-readiness workstream when v2 transitions from prototype to production. This anchors PI-017.
after_payload.rationale: For v0.5: scope is 'make multiple engagements possible,' not 'redesign the API layer.' Option A delivers the former without doing the latter. The API server code stays as-shipped in v0.4; engagement-routing complexity lives in the desktop's launcher and switcher logic, where new code is going anyway. Consistent with DEC-039's framing ('one v2 instance per engagement, separate SQLite, separate API port'). For the longer term: kill-relaunch is intentional for the v0.x prototype phase but inappropriate for production. Cross-engagement queries, dashboards, mid-flight switching all become desirable as v2 stabilises. Committing to the migration at the prototype-to-production transition avoids the PI-001 anti-pattern (friction triggers that never fire) while keeping the v0.5 scope bounded. The trigger is a planned transition rather than friction signal.
after_payload.title: v0.5 API and MCP server model: one process per engagement bound at spawn; commitment to multi-tenant migration at prototype-to-production transition

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Global identifier scope across all engagements (CBM's first session is SES-025+). Rejected as obviously wrong on review/auditability grounds. Engagement identifiers scoped per-engagement (so the dogfood and CBM both have ENG-001). Rejected because engagement records live in the meta DB and there is no per-engagement scope to apply at this level — the meta DB is the single sequence's home.
after_payload.consequences: No changes to the v0.4 next-identifier helpers themselves; they continue to query the active DB connection and naturally return per-engagement sequences. A new next-identifier helper GET /engagements/next-identifier is added to the v0.5 build, served from the meta DB. CBM's first session record (whenever CBM Phase 1 runs) is SES-001. The dogfood engagement's sessions continue at SES-026+. Identifier collisions across engagements are accepted as not-actually-collisions: they refer to distinct records in distinct engagement scopes.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q5. With per-engagement DBs (DEC-078, DEC-079), the question is whether governance identifiers (SES-NNN, DEC-NNN, PI-NNN, etc.) and methodology identifiers (DOM-NNN, ENT-NNN, PROC-NNN, CRMC-NNN) sequence globally across all engagements or per-engagement. CBM's first session being SES-025 because the dogfood happens to have 24 prior sessions would be absurd; per-engagement scope is the only sane choice for review/auditability. The v0.4 next-identifier helpers (from DEC-068 slice A) query the active DB connection — so per-engagement scope happens automatically without v0.5 helper changes. The remaining question is mostly about confirming the obvious answer.
after_payload.decision: Governance and methodology identifiers (SES, DEC, PI, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT, PROC, CRMC) scope per-engagement: each engagement's DB has its own identifier sequences. CBM's first session is SES-001 within the CBM engagement, independent of the dogfood's SES-025+. Engagement identifiers (ENG-NNN) scope to the meta DB: one sequence per v2 install. On Doug's laptop, the dogfood is ENG-001, CBM is ENG-002, etc. Reinstalling v2 from scratch and recreating engagements resets the ENG sequence; engagement_code values remain the stable human-facing handle.
after_payload.rationale: Per-engagement scope for governance/methodology is the only sane choice. The v0.4 next-identifier helpers query the active DB connection, so per-engagement scope is automatic with no v0.5 changes to the helpers themselves. Engagement identifiers naturally scope to the meta DB because that is where engagement records live. Identifier collisions across engagements don't matter: engagement A's DEC-001 and engagement B's DEC-001 are distinct decisions in distinct engagements, related only by external context. The meta DB has its own next-identifier helper (GET /engagements/next-identifier) following the v0.4 pattern.
after_payload.title: Identifier scope: per-engagement for governance and methodology identifiers; engagement identifiers scoped to meta DB
