after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B — eager at v2 launch. Rejected on correlated-failure-risk grounds and on cost-for-engagements-not-used grounds. Option C — explicit only. Rejected because it creates friction for normal use without a clear safety win; well-tested Alembic migrations applied lazily at activation are the standard pattern.
after_payload.consequences: v0.5 build adds a helper (call it run_engagement_migrations) that takes an engagement DB path and applies pending migrations. The desktop's activation sequence calls this helper before spawning the API subprocess (Step 3 in multi-engagement-architecture.md §4's 12-step sequence). The activation UX shows an 'Upgrading engagement database...' indicator when migrations are running; for an engagement already at head, the helper is nearly free. Forward-only migrations: same posture as v0.4. The meta DB's own Alembic chain applies at desktop launch — meta DB cannot be opened if its migrations have failed. Acceptance criterion: 'open a stale engagement DB with a newer codebase; activation applies pending migrations; engagement opens with schema at head.'
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q6. When a new Alembic migration ships (e.g., v0.6 adds a column to the domain table for domain_short_code per PI-007), each engagement's DB needs the migration applied. Three viable options: (A) lazy at engagement-open — apply pending migrations when the engagement is activated; (B) eager at v2 launch — apply pending migrations to all engagements at desktop startup; (C) explicit only — never automatic, user runs a CLI command. Affects operational UX after every release that ships a migration. v1 uses Option A (run_client_migrations runs at activation per v1's ActiveClientContext).
after_payload.decision: Lazy at engagement-open. When an engagement is activated, the desktop launcher runs alembic upgrade head against that engagement's DB before spawning the API subprocess. Mirrors v1's run_client_migrations pattern. Each engagement's alembic_version table tracks its own head; no cross-engagement coupling. Engagements that are never opened never get migrated; engagements that are opened after a long pause apply pending migrations at activation. The meta DB has its own Alembic chain that applies at desktop launch (before any engagement is listable) — failure to migrate the meta DB is a hard-fail UX.
after_payload.rationale: Matches v1's proven pattern. Defers work to when it is actually needed; never-opened engagements pay no cost. Failure modes are scoped to one engagement at a time — a broken migration affects activation of one engagement, not the entire v2 install or all engagements at startup. Eager-at-launch (Option B) creates correlated failure risk (every engagement fails if the migration has a bug, blocking the desktop from launching) and pays cost for engagements the user isn't using. Explicit-only (Option C) creates friction for normal use without a clear win — Alembic migrations applied at known checkpoints (release boundaries) are well-understood, and the engine should 'just work' when the codebase has moved forward.
after_payload.title: Migrations across engagements: lazy at engagement-open via run_engagement_migrations helper

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B — dual-path compatibility (engine reads from both v2.db and engagements/CRMBUILDER.db forever). Rejected because it accrues permanent technical debt in every code path that opens the dogfood, defeats the Q2 convention, and creates a forever-special-case for the dogfood that the workstream plan explicitly does not want ('the dogfood should be one engagement among potentially several'). Option C — default-engagement special case with engagement_db_path field on the engagement record pointing at the old path. Rejected because it would require reopening Q2's no-path-field decision and would compromise the Q2 convention with a permanent exception just for the dogfood.
after_payload.consequences: v0.5 build includes a self-contained migration module invoked by the launcher at first-launch detection of the migration-needed state. Slice placement (Slice A foundation versus a dedicated migration slice) is explicitly a Conversation 2 question per the workstream plan §10. Failure recovery: the .pre-v0.5-backup file is the recovery point; the user is told to revert by deleting the new files and reverting code to the prior v2 release. All eight tracked tables plus topics and risks have row counts in the new path matching the source. Idempotency: rerunning after successful migration is a no-op. The dogfood engagement record (CRMBUILDER) is born at migration time with all the field values specified in this decision; engagement.md §3 details the schema and the engagement record values are documented in multi-engagement-architecture.md §3.7 and engagement.md.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q7. The existing crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db file (which v0.4 ships with) holds the v2-build's own governance data: 24 sessions, 74 decisions, 16 PIs, 66 references, 222 change_log entries, 42 catalog entries, 13 status versions, 2 charter versions, plus topics and risks. v0.5 needs to retrofit this into the new engagement model without losing or corrupting any of it. Three options: (A) one-shot explicit migration with backup-first / verify-after / then-delete discipline; (B) dual-path compatibility (engine knows both old and new paths for the dogfood forever); (C) default-engagement special case (the dogfood's path is allowed to live outside engagements/ via a configurable engagement_db_path field). Coupled to DEC-079 (Q2 chose convention; B and C compromise that retroactively).
after_payload.decision: One-shot explicit migration runs at v0.5 first launch when the engine detects an existing crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db and a missing or empty meta DB. The migration is idempotent on rerun. Operations: (1) Backup v2.db to crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db.pre-v0.5-backup (left in place; user can delete manually after confirming v0.5 works). (2) Create the meta DB at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db and run its initial Alembic migration to head. (3) INSERT the CRMBUILDER engagement row with the v0.5 migration's specified field values (engagement_identifier=ENG-001, engagement_code=CRMBUILDER, engagement_name='CRM Builder v2', engagement_purpose='Dogfood instance hosting the v2 build's own governance content (sessions, decisions, planning items, methodology catalog).', engagement_status=active, engagement_export_dir=absolute path of PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ computed from engine repo root, audit timestamps set to migration time). (4) Copy v2.db to crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db. (5) Open the new path; query expected row counts for sessions, decisions, planning_items, refs, change_log, charter, status, base_entity_catalog, topics, risks; each count must match the source. (6) Delete the original v2.db (the .pre-v0.5-backup copy remains). (7) Refresh the JSON snapshots at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ from the new path. Three install scenarios handled: existing v2 install (migrate), fresh install with no v2.db (no-op; meta DB created empty; first-launch UX guides creating engagements explicitly), rerun after successful migration (detect already-migrated state and exit cleanly).
after_payload.rationale: Q2 chose convention (DEC-079); Q7 respects Q2's choice rather than carve out a permanent exception. The dogfood deserves the same convention every future engagement gets — both for consistency and because every line of 'is this the dogfood path or the new path?' logic is debt the codebase pays forever. The migration is a one-time cost; the special-case paths in Options B and C are a forever cost. Backup-first / verify-row-counts / then-delete discipline is well-understood standard discipline for destructive migrations, not novel risk.
after_payload.title: Dogfood migration: one-shot explicit migration moves v2.db to engagements/CRMBUILDER.db at v0.5 first launch

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Option B — all in engine repo organized by code (db-export/engagements/{code}/). Rejected because reviewers of a CBM engagement's lineage would have to look in two repos (CBM for PRDs and process docs, engine repo for JSON snapshots), defeating the natural-home principle. Option C — manual command only for client engagements (no auto-export for client engagements; dogfood-only auto-export). Rejected because it creates a two-class engagement model where the dogfood is special, contradicting the workstream plan's framing that the dogfood should be one engagement among potentially several.
after_payload.consequences: engagement_export_dir nullable TEXT column added to the meta DB engagements table. The dogfood migration sets this field for the CRMBUILDER row to the computed absolute path of PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/. The New-Engagement dialog (built in v0.5 Slice B) includes an engagement_export_dir field (optional; user can leave blank and set later via Edit). v0.4's export hook is rebound to read the active engagement's value. Drift handling: if the path is set but doesn't exist or isn't writable, the export attempt logs a warning and skips; user fixes via Edit Engagement. Null values produce a log warning at activation and no file writes. Absolute paths in the meta DB are fine because the meta DB is gitignored data; machine moves require fresh install and re-creation of engagements, which re-sets paths.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q8. v0.4's db-export JSON snapshots at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ are the git-tracked render of the dogfood's content. For v0.5, the question is where each engagement's exports go. Three options: (A) split — dogfood exports stay in the engine repo; client engagement exports land in the client repo via a path field on the engagement record; (B) all in engine repo organized by code (reviewers would have to look in two places for a client engagement's documents); (C) manual command only for client engagements (creates a two-class engagement model). Coupled to Q2 (which chose convention — no path field for DBs) — Q8 accepts an asymmetry because DB files are gitignored data while exports are git-tracked documents.
after_payload.decision: Exports for each engagement land where the engagement's other documents live. A nullable engagement_export_dir column is added to the meta DB engagements table (TEXT, validated when set: must be an existing writable directory; absolute filesystem path). Dogfood's value is set by the v0.5 dogfood migration (DEC-084) to the absolute path of PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ (computed from the engine repo root). Client engagements set their value at creation time via the New-Engagement dialog (user browses to the client repo or types the path). Export-refresh writes to the active engagement's engagement_export_dir; null disables auto-export with a log warning at activation. The existing v0.4 export hook is rebound to read the active engagement's engagement_export_dir.
after_payload.rationale: Each engagement's git-tracked lineage belongs with the engagement's other documents, where reviewers will naturally look. For the dogfood, that's the engine repo (where the v2-build's PRDs and methodology documents live); for client engagements (CBM, future clients), that's the client repo (where the engagement's process docs, entity PRDs, etc. live). Forcing all exports into a centralised engine-repo location would split each client engagement's documentation between two repos. The asymmetry with Q2 (DB files in engine repo) is defensible because DB files are gitignored data and exports are git-tracked documents — different concerns, different homes.
after_payload.title: Per-engagement exports: split via nullable engagement_export_dir field on the meta DB engagement record

after_payload.alternatives_considered: For status lifecycle: Option B (domain pattern candidate/confirmed/deferred with one-way gate). Rejected because engagement records aren't proposed-then-verified; adopting the domain pattern would create awkward UX ('why is my engagement in candidate state? Who verifies it?'). Option C (active/archived, two states). Rejected because it drops the 'temporarily on hold' state which has real operational meaning; users would either keep paused engagements 'active' and remember they're on hold (fragile) or repeatedly archive/unarchive (friction). For the field set: adding engagement_description, engagement_notes, or engagement_db_path was considered for each. Rejected case-by-case as described in the decision text. For the UI shape: a ListDetailPanel in a new 'Engagements' sidebar group was a default candidate; the conversation surfaced that engagement is structurally different and deferred the exact visual layout to Conversation 2 with explicit deviation rationale.
after_payload.consequences: The meta DB's engagements table has the ten columns specified. The Alembic migration for the meta DB creates this table at v0.5 first launch (or via the dogfood migration's step 2 per DEC-084). v0.5 build (Conversation 2 + Claude Code slices) implements: the access-layer methods (list_engagements, get_engagement, create_engagement, update_engagement, patch_engagement, delete_engagement, restore_engagement, next_engagement_identifier), the eight REST endpoints, the meta-DB connection handling in the API server (the two-database server detail per multi-engagement-architecture.md §3.10), and the engagement UI (switching and management affordances; exact layout from Conversation 2). The dogfood migration sets engagement_status=active for the CRMBUILDER row. New engagements created via the New-Engagement dialog default to active. Free transitions between active/paused/archived are accepted; transitions to values outside the enum return 422 invalid_enum_value. Soft-delete via DELETE sets engagement_deleted_at without touching the per-engagement DB file; restore via POST /restore clears it. Acceptance criteria: 12 testable statements in engagement.md §3.7 covering schema migration, identifier/code constraint enforcement, status enum and transition validation, export_dir validation, access-layer methods, REST endpoints, soft-delete/restore round-trip, meta-DB serving, engagement_last_opened_at updates on activation, and the dogfood/CBM sample-record scenario.
after_payload.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q10 and Q10b. The engagement entity type is the sole new methodology entity in v0.5. Q10 asks for the field set; Q10b asks for the status lifecycle. The kickoff's weak prior for fields (engagement_identifier, engagement_code mirroring v1, engagement_name, engagement_purpose, engagement_status, audit timestamps, possibly engagement_data_dir if Q2 settles on configurable paths) plus three lifecycle options (domain pattern, active/paused/archived, active/archived). Coupled to all prior decisions in this conversation: DEC-078 (engagement record lives in meta DB), DEC-079 (no engagement_db_path), DEC-080 (engagement_last_opened_at column), DEC-082 (ENG identifier scope), DEC-085 (engagement_export_dir column).
after_payload.decision: The engagement entity type has these properties. Identifier prefix: ENG (3 letters; no collision with existing prefixes; format ENG-NNN zero-padded to 3 digits; server-assigned on POST omission; helper at GET /engagements/next-identifier). Storage: meta DB engagements table at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db. Fields (all prefixed engagement_ per DEC-046): engagement_identifier (TEXT, required, server-assigned, ^ENG-\d{3}$, unique), engagement_code (TEXT, required, regex ^[A-Z][A-Z0-9]{1,9}$ mirroring v1's Client.code exactly, case-insensitive unique within the meta DB), engagement_name (TEXT, required, case-insensitive unique), engagement_purpose (TEXT, required, plain text 1-2 sentences), engagement_status (TEXT, required, default 'active', enum {active, paused, archived}, free transitions), engagement_last_opened_at (DATETIME, nullable, per DEC-080), engagement_export_dir (TEXT, nullable absolute path validated when set, per DEC-085), engagement_created_at / engagement_updated_at / engagement_deleted_at (inherited base behavior). Three deliberate omissions from the domain.md pattern: no engagement_description (purpose suffices), no engagement_notes (engagement is routing metadata not methodology content; scratchpad notes belong inside the per-engagement DB), no engagement_db_path (DEC-079 chose convention). Status lifecycle: active/paused/archived with free transitions, default starter 'active'. Engagement records are direct creations (not propose-verified by a client), so domain's one-way candidate→confirmed gate doesn't fit. Three states capture real operational lifecycle (active work, on hold, wound up); free transitions match reality (engagements can resume from any state). Soft-delete independent of status. No relationships in v0.5. API surface: standard endpoint set (list, get, create, full replace, partial update, soft-delete, restore, next-identifier) served from the meta DB. No activate endpoint — activation is desktop-side orchestration per multi-engagement-architecture.md §4. UI shape: deviates from the spec guide's default Methodology-sidebar-group placement because engagement is structurally different (v2-install-level routing metadata, not domain-scope content); two needed affordances (switching UI and management UI); exact visual layout deferred to Conversation 2 in coordination with the styling workstream (PI-001) per DEC-076's boundary discipline. Full spec at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-schema-specs/engagement.md with 12 acceptance criteria in §3.7.
after_payload.rationale: Field set follows DEC-046 (parent-prefix naming) and the established methodology-entity-schema-design conventions, adapted for engagement's structural difference. engagement_code mirroring v1's Client.code exactly (^[A-Z][A-Z0-9]{1,9}$, length 2-10) lets v1 and v2 co-reference the same engagement by code if useful later. The three omissions (description, notes, db_path) are justified by engagement's nature as routing metadata rather than methodology content. Lifecycle: engagement records are direct creations not propose-verified, so domain's one-way gate doesn't fit (Option B). Three states capture the real operational lifecycle without ceremony; two states (Option C) drops 'paused' which has real-world meaning. Free transitions match reality (an engagement on hold can resume; an archived engagement can be reactivated if a client comes back). UI deviation justified by engagement's structural difference from the four v0.4 methodology entities; the Methodology sidebar group is for domain-scope content, not for v2-install-level routing metadata, and engagement needs two distinct affordances (always-visible switcher and occasional-access manager) rather than one master/detail panel.
after_payload.title: Engagement entity schema, identifier, status lifecycle, and API surface

after_payload.description:
Anchored by DEC-081. v0.5 ships one API and MCP subprocess per engagement (Option A); multi-tenant single-process server (Option B) is the target architecture for v2's production phase. The migration lands when v2 transitions from prototype (v0.x releases) to production (v1.0). Trigger is a planned transition, not a friction signal (avoiding the PI-001 anti-pattern of friction triggers that never fire).

Scope of the migration when it lands:

1. Refactor every REST endpoint to accept engagement context. Two viable mechanisms: a request header (X-Engagement: ENG-NNN) or a path-prefix (/v1/engagements/ENG-NNN/sessions, etc.). The mechanism is settled when the workstream opens; both options have precedent in similar systems.

2. Refactor the access-layer connection model to hold per-engagement connections. Options include: a connection pool keyed by engagement_identifier opened lazily on first use; eager pool warming at startup; or per-request connection acquire/release with engagement-context binding. The pool strategy is settled by the workstream.

3. Refactor tests for engagement-context fixtures. Every test that today asserts behavior against a single DB needs to express which engagement it operates against. v0.4's test suite is the regression net.

4. Reshape the MCP server to mirror the multi-tenant API. MCP tools either gain an engagement-context input argument or a per-tool engagement selector. Specific shape is settled by the workstream.

5. Remove the kill-relaunch dance from the desktop's engagement-switch path. The desktop's ActiveEngagementContext continues to exist but switching becomes a fast in-process operation rather than a process-lifecycle dance. The 'Switching engagement...' UX indicator becomes vestigial or is removed.

6. Consider adding an activate API endpoint (POST /engagements/{identifier}/activate) at this time. The endpoint becomes possible once the API server can switch engagements without restarting; whether to expose it depends on the workstream's posture on cross-engagement scripting use cases.

Dependencies and ordering: PI-017's workstream is sequenced after v0.5 ships and after enough real-use signal has accumulated (CBM Phase 1 underway or completed; possibly additional client engagements in flight) to validate that the prototype-to-production transition is the right moment. The exact timing is determined at the production-readiness workstream's opening kickoff conversation, not pre-committed here.

Note: this PI is not the same as the deferred 'cross-engagement reporting' or 'engagement-level access control' workstreams. Those are separate v0.6+ candidates. PI-017 is strictly the infrastructure migration; cross-engagement features become technically feasible after PI-017 ships but are out of PI-017's scope.
after_payload.title: Migrate API and MCP servers to multi-tenant model

after_payload.artifacts_produced: Three documents committed to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ across two commits on origin/main: (1) styling-design-pass.md (~709 lines, new) — the design pass document; (2) close-out-payloads/ses_027.json (this payload) — SES-027 session record, DEC-087 through DEC-094 decision records, eight decided_in references; (3) prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-027.md — the apply prompt that delivers SES-027 + DEC-087 through DEC-094 + references via Claude Code, mirroring the SES-025 apply-prompt structure. No PI patches this time. The standard apply_close_out.py handles all three sections (session, decisions, references) idempotently.
after_payload.conversation_reference: Claude.ai conversation on 05-16-26 that opened against the styling Conversation 1 kickoff prompt (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-conversation-1-kickoff.md, committed earlier same day as part of SES-025 closeout). The conversation drove a structured visual-design discussion through the foundation token layer (density, color, typography, radius/border, elevation, icon library) and through the component visual decisions (sidebar, panel chrome, master pane, detail pane, buttons, form controls, dialogs, About, status/error/warning surfaces, crash banner). Each consequential decision used the eight-element decision template; smaller derivative values were drafted and announced; the design pass document was built incrementally as decisions confirmed. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
after_payload.in_flight_at_end: Styling Conversation 2 (build planning) queued in a fresh Claude.ai chat — takes styling-design-pass.md as input, produces a styling release PRD, an implementation plan with slice breakdown, and Claude Code slice build prompts. Also settles the version-bundling question (ship as v0.5 bundled with engagement management, or as v0.6 separately) deferred from the workstream plan. v0.5 Conversation 1 (engagement management) may also still be running in parallel — see v0.5-conversation-1-kickoff.md; the two workstreams are coupled only at v0.5's engagement panel (which inherits whatever design tokens have shipped at slice-land time). After both Conversation 2's close, Claude Code slice prompts execute the build; styling build slices follow the strawman five-slice structure in workstream plan §5.3 (Foundation / Governance retrofit / Methodology retrofit / Dialog polish / Closeout). The paper-test (DEC-077) stays deferred until v0.5 ships and a CBM engagement record exists.
after_payload.title: Styling Conversation 1 — design pass: tokens, component visual decisions, application priorities, acceptance criteria
after_payload.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: the styling Conversation 1 kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-conversation-1-kickoff.md (authored 05-16-26 14:00 during the SES-025 closeout), against the project default ARCHITECTURE operating mode. The kickoff framed Conversation 1 as design-only — no code, no build planning, no version-bundling settling, no work on engagement management (v0.5 Conversation 1 runs in parallel and owns the data/routing layer). Target deliverable: a single design pass document at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-design-pass.md covering design tokens, component visual decisions, application priorities, and acceptance criteria. The conversation was explicitly framed as visual-iteration-heavy rather than written-PRD-heavy — multiple short rounds (propose → react → revise → propose next) rather than one long writeup.

Pre-flight. Sparse clone of crmbuilder repo with CLAUDE.md, PRDs/, and crmbuilder-v2/ source. Read CLAUDE.md (684 lines) to ground architecture and conventions. Verified SES-025 close-out had applied cleanly: latest session SES-025, latest decision DEC-077, planning items through PI-016. Working tree clean on main. Next available identifiers: SES-027, DEC-087, PI-017. Read the styling workstream plan at styling-workstream-plan.md (227 lines) end to end. Read the v0.4 UI PRD §§4.1-4.7 (panel UI specifications) and §6 (acceptance criteria), and the v0.3 UI PRD §4.1 (ListDetailPanel factory refactor section) to ground the structural pattern that the visual treatment applies to. Pulled DEC-024 / DEC-026 / DEC-037 / DEC-042 / DEC-075 / DEC-076 / DEC-077 from the decisions snapshot — the historical deferral chain (four deferrals through v0.4) plus the parallel-workstream reopening (DEC-076).

Current visual state inventory. Grepped crmbuilder-v2/src for setStyleSheet/QPalette/stylesheet/color/background/font-family/font-size hits — 66 total touchpoints across the v2 source. Three recurring patterns emerged: read-only field treatment (color: #444; background: #f4f4f4) used identically across every methodology panel (Domains, Entities, Processes, CRM Candidates) and base/crud_dialog.py — the closest thing v2 has to a shipping convention. Secondary-text labels (color: #888) used on Sessions, Risks, Topics, Decisions, Planning Items panels for empty-state messages and captions. Navy stub (#1F3864) used in two visible-to-user places: the sidebar stale-indicator dot (via QPainter on a QPixmap) and the error-dialog banner title text. Plus one-off treatments: destructive-button red (#c1272d / disabled #b6868a) on reference-delete and crud-dialog delete buttons; inline error red (#B22222) on validation errors; warning red (same #B22222) on the Processes panel's soft-deleted-domain warning. The crash banner has its own self-contained chrome with white-on-color text — the only branded surface in v0.4. The sidebar (sidebar.py) has zero visual styling: group headers are non-selectable list items with Qt defaults. The About dialog is pure Qt defaults: QFormLayout with bold labels, 16px margins, 12px spacing, 440px min width, no chrome. Both blank canvases for the design pass.

Proposed decision order. Token-layer decisions in upstream-to-downstream sequence: density target (upstream of all spacing tokens), token-naming structure (upstream of color-token naming), brand accent + palette family (upstream of selected-state and primary-button decisions), font family (upstream of all text rendering), radius/border (smaller), elevation/depth (modal-disambiguation), icon library (parallel). Then component visual decisions in container-then-atomic order: sidebar, panel chrome, master pane, detail pane, buttons, form controls, dialogs, About, status/error/warning surfaces, crash banner reconciliation. Then application priorities and acceptance criteria. Estimated 5-10 consequential pause-decisions plus many decide-and-announce drafts.

Decision 1 — Density target. First pause, full eight-element template. Three options: compact (~20-22px row heights, 13px body, IDE-aesthetic — VS Code / JetBrains feel), default (~26-28px rows, 14px body, Qt-native-aligned), comfortable (~30-34px rows, 14-16px body, consumer-SaaS feel). Recommendation was compact, on the reasoning that v2 is a power-user tool with one primary user (Doug) spending substantial daily time inside it; compact recovers screen real estate that pays back in cumulative scan time. The recommendation was overridden by Doug's workstation context: 3× 4K monitors, rarely uses a laptop. At 4K HiDPI, compact density would feel undersized rather than crisp (HiDPI scaling already tightens visual rhythm), and there is no scarcity of screen pixels to optimize for. Default density chosen. Spacing scale fell out as follow-on: 4px base unit with scale 4/8/12/16/20/24/32/40/48; list row 28px; form field 28px; button padding 6×14px with 88px minimum width; panel chrome 16px outer padding; dialog margins 20px outer + 12px between rows + 16px above buttons.

Decision 2 — Token-naming structure. The kickoff flagged dark-mode anticipation at the token-naming layer as a real fork, with weak prior to anticipate. The workstream plan §3.2 explicitly puts dark mode implementation out of scope, deferred to a separate PI if anyone asks. Treated as decide-and-announce rather than full pause: theme-keyed dict structure (TOKENS['light'][key]) over flat tokens. Reasoning: we are building the tokens module from scratch in the build phase; adding the theme-key dict layer costs one extra dict key and one accessor function (~5 LOC) today. The retrofit cost if dark mode ever ships and tokens went flat is non-trivial (rename every color token in code, refactor every consumer). The theme-keyed structure also pays an immediate non-dark-mode dividend by separating data from interface, making future palette experiments (CBM-branded variant, print-friendly variant) trivially additive. No pushback.

Decision 3 — Brand accent and palette family. Pause with full eight-element template. Four options: keep navy #1F3864 (the legacy stub, never deliberately chosen), different blue family (steelier #1F5FBF or brighter Tailwind-like #2563EB), non-blue accent (deep green / warm purple / muted plum), grayscale-only with no chroma accent. Doug chose B (different blue). Follow-up sub-pick: steelier vs brighter — chose the steelier #1F5FBF on the reasoning that it matches the 'serious tool, deliberate choice' read better than the Tailwind-bright value, which leans consumer-SaaS-marketing. Cool grays follow naturally for pairing with cool blue accent. Drafted 9-step neutral scale: neutral.0 #FFFFFF, neutral.50 #F7F9FB, neutral.100 #EEF1F5, neutral.200 #DDE3EA, neutral.300 #C1CAD4, neutral.500 #7A8694, neutral.700 #3F4854, neutral.800 #272D36, neutral.900 #0F1318. The legacy four ad-hoc grays (#f4f4f4, #444, #666, #888) map to the new scale during the styling-build retrofit. Status colors drafted to harmonize with the cool blue accent: danger #C0392B, danger.text #A8281C, warning #B0731A, success #2D7A4D (rare in v2). No pushback on the cool-gray scale or status drafts.

Decision 4 — Font family. Pause with full eight-element template. Four options: system-default per platform (status quo), Inter Variable bundled, other open sans (Geist / IBM Plex Sans / Source Sans / JetBrains Sans), distinctive font (Berkeley Mono / Atkinson Hyperlegible). Recommended Inter; Doug chose B. Drafted size scale: caption 12px / small 13px / body 14px / body_large 16px / heading_3 18px / heading_2 22px / heading_1 28px (reserved). Weight scale (Inter Variable axis): regular 400 / medium 500 / semibold 600 / bold 700. Line-height scale: tight 1.2 (headings) / normal 1.45 (body) / relaxed 1.6 (long-form prose). Monospace: JetBrains Mono Variable bundled for identifier columns, code surfaces, About dialog path values. All loaded via QFontDatabase.addApplicationFont() at app startup. Total binary cost ~530 KB.

Decision 5 — Elevation and depth posture. Pause with full eight-element template. Three options: fully flat throughout (current behavior, no shadows), elevation only on modals (one elevation tier, applied via QGraphicsDropShadowEffect which is Qt's built-in effect — within the workstream plan's QSS-supplemented-by-built-in-effects posture, not custom paintEvent which is out of scope), broader elevation tiers (modals + popup combos + selected list rows + focused buttons — multiple z-tiers). Recommended B; Doug chose B. Drafted values: shadow.dialog at Y-offset 4px, X-offset 0px, blur radius 16px, color color.neutral.900 at 25% alpha. Plus overlay.modal_backdrop — full-window overlay at color.neutral.900 8% alpha, fade-in ~150ms when modal opens. Single elevation token applied uniformly to every QDialog subclass.

Decision 6 — Icon library. Pause, compressed template (narrower than prior token-layer decisions). Three options: Qt-built-in / system-theme icons via QIcon::fromTheme (inconsistent on Linux, empty on macOS/Windows without explicit fallback), Phosphor (~7,000 icons, 6 weight variants, MIT, warmer rounded aesthetic), Lucide (~1,400 icons, single regular stroke, ISC, minimal/restrained aesthetic). Recommended C; Doug chose C. Drafted bundling approach: store SVGs at crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/ui/assets/icons/lucide/{kebab-case-name}.svg; small loader helper wraps QSvgRenderer to produce QIcon at requested size with runtime color tinting via the design token system. Initial wave of ~10-15 icons covering Edit, Delete, Restore, Go-to-references, Copy, Add, Close, chevrons, alert, error, check.

Section 1 of the design pass document (tokens) was now substantially complete and the design pass file was created on disk at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-design-pass.md with §1.1 through §1.6 filled in or drafted, §2 stubbed for component decisions, §3 and §4 stubbed. The file was committed-ready in the container's working clone; no commits or pushes yet.

Decision 7 — Sidebar visual treatment. Pause, compressed template. The central decision was selected-entry treatment; the rest of the sidebar fell out as drafts. Three options for selected state: tinted background only (no accent bar — risk of blurring with hover), full-fill with accent color and white text (heavy in a 12+ item list), left-edge accent bar + tinted background (modern dev-tool convention — VS Code / JetBrains / Linear / shadcn). Recommended A (left bar); Doug chose A. Drafted sidebar specifics: container background color.neutral.100 distinct from panel color.neutral.50 with 1px hairline right border; sidebar width 220px; group headers in font.size.caption font.weight.semibold color.neutral.500 with +0.04em letter-spacing; entries with 32px height, font.size.body, padding space.2 × space.3; hover at color.neutral.200 background with no bar (distinct from selected); focused state matches selected plus 1px focus ring; stale-indicator dot retained at 8px circle but color flips from legacy navy to color.accent.default. No pushback on drafts.

§2.2 Panel chrome — drafted with one small announce-decision (no panel header — sidebar already indicates which panel is active, adding a header strip would be redundant labeling and consume vertical space; pushback welcome but none received). Drafts: panel content background color.neutral.50; outer padding space.4; master/detail split via existing QSplitter at 45/55 default proportion, space.3 handle width with color.neutral.100 background and color.neutral.300 hairline divider down the center.

Decision 8 — Master pane visual posture. Pause, full eight-element template. Three options: minimal/quiet (Linear-issue-list aesthetic — no banding, no dividers, header has subtle background, hover and selected tints only), structured/banded (admin-tool aesthetic — alternating row shading, row dividers, prominent header background, sidebar-mirrored selected state with left bar), middle (no banding but row dividers, clear header background, sidebar-mirrored selected state). Recommended C; Doug chose C. Reasoning: v2's master panes have low row counts (Decisions 77, References ~150, Sessions 25, others <30) and ~22 rows visible at default density on 4K — alternating banding is most valuable at 50+ visible rows where the eye loses its place; for v2 it would be visual noise. Going fully minimal would feel under-organized for a structured data table; middle threads the needle. Drafted specifics: row height 28px; row dividers 1px hairline color.neutral.200; column header background color.neutral.100 with bottom hairline color.neutral.200 and font.weight.semibold color.neutral.700 text; row hover color.neutral.100; row selected gets 3px left accent bar + color.accent.subtle background + color.neutral.900 medium-weight text (matches sidebar selected vocabulary — same design vocabulary across both surfaces, custom QStyledItemDelegate subclass shared across all panels); soft-deleted rows render in color.neutral.500 text with leading Lucide trash-2 icon at 14px in identifier column; empty state centered with primary 'No records yet' line and per-panel secondary hint line below in color.neutral.500. Identifier column renders in font.family.mono (JetBrains Mono) at font.size.small so identifiers like DEC-077 align cleanly by numeric suffix. Tree view variant (Topics panel) inherits the same treatment with Lucide chevron-right/chevron-down at 12px replacing Qt's default branch indicator and space.4 indentation per level.

Decision 9 — Detail pane. Pause, compressed (mostly draftable). The pause focused on ReferencesSection visual treatment; the smaller question of form-row label placement (label-above vs label-left) was decide-and-announce (chose label-above on the reasoning that Default density gives enough vertical room and label-above scans faster when labels vary in length — 'Identifier' vs 'Process classification'). ReferencesSection options: sub-sectioned plain list with kind header + entry rows below (A); card-style per relationship kind with bordered cards stacking vertically (B); badge/pill rendering per entry with kind as tiny caption (C). Recommended A; Doug chose A. Reasoning: ReferencesSection is reference material the user scans, not a primary editing surface — plain lists scan fastest, scale gracefully to many references, and do not compete visually with the rest of the detail pane. Cards over-bound a section already inside a well-bounded detail pane; pills compress too aggressively for typical entry length. Drafted entry rendering: identifier in font.family.mono at font.size.small color.neutral.700; title text after identifier in font.size.body color.neutral.800; space.3 between identifier and title; space.1 vertical padding per row; hover tint color.neutral.50; right-click context menu with Delete reference / Go to {target identifier}. Add-reference button as text-only with Lucide plus icon + label 'Add reference' in color.accent.default. Drafted remaining detail-pane elements: form-row label in font.size.small font.weight.medium color.neutral.700 with space.1 below before field; field height 28px; row spacing space.3; required-field marker as Lucide asterisk at 10px color.danger.text immediately after label text (no 'Required' word); editable field state coverage (default / focused / error / disabled); read-only field treatment (color.neutral.100 background, no border, color.neutral.700 text — visually distinct from disabled which has color.neutral.200 border + color.neutral.300 text); status combo with valid-transitions hint caption below; notes collapsible toggle with Lucide chevron + label; section grouping space.6 between major sections with no dividers; action button row anchored at bottom with right-aligned Cancel + Save and left-aligned Delete.

§2.5 Buttons — fully drafted, no pause needed. Five categories × five states. Primary (color.accent.default fill with color.neutral.0 text), Secondary (transparent with color.neutral.300 border), Destructive (color.danger.default fill — retires the legacy #c1272d and disabled #b6868a treatments), Text/Link (transparent with color.accent.default text in font.weight.medium for 'Add reference' and similar affordances), Icon-only (28×28 with color.neutral.700 icon at rest, color.neutral.100 hover background, tooltip required). All buttons share padding space.1 × space.3 (effectively 6×14), radius.subtle, minimum width 88px so Save/Cancel line up in dialog button rows. Per-state visuals fully specified.

§2.6 Form controls — fully drafted, no pause needed. Text input, multi-line text (80px minimum height), combo box with Lucide chevron-down right-side affordance and full dropdown popup treatment (color.accent.subtle background on current value, color.neutral.100 on hover), checkbox (16×16 box with color.accent.default fill and Lucide check icon when checked), radio button reserved (no current v2 surface uses), date/time picker reserved (no current v2 surface uses), spin box reserved, scrollbars OS-default per kickoff settled-by-convention guidance, tab order top-to-bottom with initial focus on first editable field not Save button.

§2.7 Dialogs — drafted with one announce-decision. The substantive question was whether dialog bodies show an internal context strip identifying the record being edited; decide-and-announce was yes for record-editing dialogs (not for create-new, About, or delete-confirm). Reasoning: OS title bar text ('Edit Decision DEC-076') is variably visible across Linux compositors and macOS focus states; a small internal strip inside the dialog body anchors the user without requiring them to look at OS chrome. Context strip drafted: anchored at top of dialog body flush with OS title bar, background color.neutral.100, padding space.3, identifier in font.family.mono at font.size.small color.neutral.700 font.weight.medium followed by space.3 horizontal then record name/title in font.size.body color.neutral.800 (truncates with ellipsis), hairline color.neutral.200 below. Remaining dialog drafts: container background color.neutral.0, inner padding space.5, radius.dialog 6px where platform renders, drop shadow per shadow.dialog and backdrop overlay per overlay.modal_backdrop. Action button row anchored at bottom with right-aligned Cancel + primary and left-aligned Destructive. Delete-confirm dialogs specifically: no context strip, single paragraph body with deletion target inline at font.line.relaxed 1.6, Destructive button right-aligned with Cancel to its left.

Decision 10 — About dialog showcase treatment. Pause, compressed template. Three options: apply design system with no special treatment (under-leverages the natural identity moment), modest showcase with wordmark + tagline header but no logo (visible identity without requiring brand-mark authoring), full showcase with custom brand mark in addition to wordmark + tagline (requires authoring a logo). Recommended B; Doug chose B. Reasoning: the About dialog is the natural identity moment in the application and treating it like every other dialog feels under-leveraged. But brand-mark authoring is a separate subjective design problem unsuitable for remote conversation resolution, and v2 has no established mark to evolve. Modest showcase threads the needle. Drafted: wordmark 'CRMBuilder v2' in font.size.heading_2 font.weight.semibold color.neutral.900; tagline below at font.size.small color.neutral.500 — proposed 'Declarative CRM deployment and methodology authoring' with alternative tagline candidates noted in §2.8 as refineable at build time. Metadata table restructured from QFormLayout to a vertical two-line-per-row list (label above value, value selectable so users can copy paths). Single secondary 'Close' button right-aligned at bottom. Minimum width preserved at 440px from current code.

§2.9 Status / error / warning surfaces — fully drafted, no pause needed. Inline form-field errors below the field at font.size.caption color.danger.text with no icon (the field's red border carries the visual signal). Inline panel-level warnings as a single-row callout with Lucide circle-alert icon at 14px in color.warning.default + warning text at font.size.small color.warning.default — the existing _WARNING_STYLE = 'color: #B22222;' on panels/processes.py updates to use color.warning.default (amber, not danger red — the soft-deleted-domain message is informational warning, not error). Error dialog (dialogs/error.py) header retoken: leading Lucide circle-x at 18px in color.danger.default + title in font.size.heading_3 font.weight.semibold color.danger.text. Retires the legacy _BANNER_STYLE = 'color: #1F3864; font-weight: bold;'. Success affirmations rare in v2 — reserved pattern with Lucide check icon and color.success.default treatment if introduced. Status indicators include the stale-dot (covered in §2.1) and reserved future status badges for singleton-selected CRM Candidates (pill rendering at radius.subtle).

§2.10 Crash banner reconciliation — decide-and-announce. Two options: re-skin into the design system (use color.danger.default from the new system, retire the bespoke navy/white treatment) versus keep outside the design system as the one 'this is exceptional' surface that doesn't follow normal app rules. Re-skin chosen. Reasoning: leaving the banner outside means permanent parallel maintenance for one surface; folding in costs nothing visually since color.danger.default is unambiguously red and reads as alarming; future style adjustments propagate automatically. Drafted: banner background color.danger.default, text color.neutral.0 in font.size.body font.weight.medium, padding space.3 vertical × space.4 horizontal, leading Lucide circle-alert at 16px in color.neutral.0. Banner buttons treated as semi-transparent white-on-color: default at 15% alpha background, hover at 30% alpha, pressed at 45% alpha. Pushback welcome but none received.

§3 Application priorities — drafted directly, no pause. Distinguishes high-attention surfaces (per-surface tuning required, custom widget code beyond QSS) from passive-absorption surfaces (QSS plus token mapping only). High attention: sidebar (custom delegate or paint-event override for selected-state left bar), master pane (shared QStyledItemDelegate for left bar + soft-deleted treatment), ReferencesSection (new layout pattern), About dialog (header block restructure from QFormLayout to QVBoxLayout), crash banner (button-state retuning), modal elevation infrastructure (QGraphicsDropShadowEffect setup + backdrop overlay widget). Passive absorption: panel chrome, detail pane form treatment, buttons, form controls, standard dialogs, inline status/error/warning treatments. Suggested implementation order across the build phase: foundation (tokens module, font loaders, icon loader, base widget refactors, elevation infrastructure, About dialog as smallest end-to-end exercise) → sidebar → master pane delegate → governance panel retrofit → methodology panel retrofit → dialog polish → status/error/warning + crash banner → closeout. Order approximate; Conversation 2 may restructure based on file-conflict topology with v0.5 parallel work.

§4 Acceptance criteria — drafted directly, no pause. Five categories: token completeness (every category in §1 has concrete values, no TBDs, theme-keyed structure internally consistent, status colors harmonize with accent), component completeness (every component class has prose visual decision, no stubs remain, state coverage complete for all interactive components, selected-state vocabulary consistent across surfaces), contradiction check (no conflicting values, density-derived sizes mutually consistent), accessibility floor (WCAG AA contrast verified for every text-on-background combination — body text 14.4:1 passes AAA, secondary 4.62:1 passes AA, accent on white 4.61:1 passes AA, white-on-accent 4.66:1 passes AA for body, white-on-danger ~5.1:1 passes AA, warning on white borderline at 12px caption size noted for build-time refinement — focus indicators visible on every interactive element, color is not the sole signal for state), implementer readiness (implementer can produce QSS without further questions, legacy-to-token mapping explicit). Out of scope documented in §4.6: dark mode color values (structure supports but values not authored), animation, icon SVG authoring beyond initial set, cross-platform polish testing (Windows-specific deferred per workstream plan), full accessibility audit beyond WCAG AA contrast floor (deferred to separate workstream if requested).

Document built incrementally during the conversation, ending at 709 lines, four main sections fully filled (§1 tokens, §2 component decisions across ten subsections, §3 application priorities, §4 acceptance criteria), preamble with revision control and change log per project standards, MM-DD-YY HH:MM timestamp format.

Close-out planning. Eight consequential decisions to formalize as DEC-087 through DEC-094: density (default), brand accent + cool-gray family, theme-keyed token structure, font stack (Inter + JetBrains Mono), modal elevation only, Lucide icon library, selected-state visual vocabulary (left accent bar + tinted background applied to both sidebar and master pane), About dialog modest-showcase treatment. Smaller decide-and-announce items (no panel header, internal dialog context strip, label-above forms, ReferencesSection sub-sectioned plain list, master pane middle posture, button drafts, form control drafts, status surface drafts, crash banner re-skin) fold into the design pass document but do not warrant separate DEC records — they derive from the eight foundational ones or from the workstream plan's pre-existing scope. No new planning items: all deferred work either folds into existing PI-001 (the parent for the whole styling pass) or is documented as out-of-scope in §4.6 of the design pass. Path 1 close-out chosen (apply prompt via Claude Code, mirroring the SES-025 precedent) over Path 2 (typing each record into the desktop New Session dialog) — apply prompt produces a reviewable artifact.

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Compact density (~20-22px rows, 13px body) — rejected because at 4K HiDPI compact reads undersized rather than dense, and Doug's workstation has no screen-real-estate scarcity to optimize for. Comfortable density (~30-34px rows, 14-16px body) — rejected because it wastes pixels at 4K and doesn't match a power-user-tool user model.
after_payload.consequences: Every spacing token in styling-design-pass.md §1.1 derives from Default density. The 4px base unit and 9-step spacing scale (0/4/8/12/16/20/24/32/40/48) inform every padding, margin, and gap decision downstream. Button geometry (6×14px padding, 88px minimum width) ensures Save/Cancel buttons line up in dialog rows. The Inter Variable body size is 14px (which holds up at Default density; would have needed 13px at compact, would have allowed 16px at comfortable). Implementer reading the design pass can produce QSS without re-deriving any spacing value.
after_payload.context: The styling Conversation 1 design pass opened against the kickoff prompt with density as the most-upstream token-level decision — it shapes every spacing token in the system. The kickoff's weak prior framed density as a real fork worth pausing on. Three concrete options were on the table: compact (~20-22px list rows, 13px body text, IDE-aesthetic — VS Code / JetBrains feel, recovers screen real estate at the cost of needing a body font that holds up at small sizes), default (~26-28px rows, 14px body, Qt-native-aligned, comfortable for daily use), comfortable (~30-34px rows, 14-16px body, consumer-SaaS feel, wastes screen on power users). v2 today renders with Qt-default density and no deliberate spacing rhythm; the design pass needed to settle this before any other token-layer work.
after_payload.decision: **Default density.** Concrete derived values: list row height 28px (master panes — QTableView, QTreeView, QListView); form field height 28px (text inputs, combos); button padding 6×14px with 88px minimum width; panel chrome 16px outer padding; inter-pane (master ↔ detail) spacing 12px; dialog margins 20px outer + 12px between rows + 16px above buttons; body text 14px. Spacing scale: 4px base unit with scale 4 / 8 / 12 / 16 / 20 / 24 / 32 / 40 / 48. See styling-design-pass.md §1.1 for the full token specification.
after_payload.rationale: The initial Claude recommendation was compact (power-user tool, single primary user, list-dense panels where dense rows recover daily scan time). The recommendation was overridden by Doug's workstation context: 3× 4K monitors at a workstation, rarely uses a laptop. At 4K HiDPI, compact density would feel undersized rather than crisp (HiDPI scaling already tightens visual rhythm relative to logical pixels), and there is no scarcity of screen pixels to optimize for. Default sits in the middle, native to Qt's own assumptions, and renders crisply across all three monitors. Comfortable was the other extreme and was rejected on the same no-scarcity logic — wasting screen on a power-user tool is the wrong direction.
after_payload.title: Density target: Default — workstation context rules out compact and comfortable

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Keep navy #1F3864 (Option A) — rejected as default-by-accident; ratifying it would skip the question of whether it's actually right. Brighter Tailwind-like blue #2563EB — rejected as consumer-SaaS-marketing leaning; doesn't match the deliberate-but-restrained design direction. Non-blue accent (Option C — green / purple / plum) — rejected for visual continuity reasons; v0.4 screenshots use navy, and a non-blue accent would feel like a wholly different app. Grayscale-only (Option D) — rejected as too restrained for a tool with frequent selection and status indication needs; chroma is required for selection signals and status colors regardless. Warm or neutral gray family — rejected for less-cohesive pairing with the cool blue accent.
after_payload.consequences: color.accent.default retires the legacy #1F3864 navy in two existing places: the sidebar's _STALE_DOT_COLOR in sidebar.py and the _BANNER_STYLE in dialogs/error.py. The four ad-hoc grays in panels/*.py (sessions, risks, topics, decisions, planning_items) and base/crud_dialog.py map to the new neutral scale during the styling build: #f4f4f4 → color.neutral.100, #666 → color.neutral.700, #888 → color.neutral.500, #444 → color.neutral.700. The destructive #c1272d (and disabled #b6868a) on reference-delete and crud-dialog delete buttons retire to color.danger.default + the disabled-gray treatment from §2.5. The _WARNING_STYLE = 'color: #B22222;' on panels/processes.py retires to color.warning.default (amber, not the legacy red — the soft-deleted-domain message is informational warning, not error). The retirement is mechanical search-and-replace in the build phase plus the token-aware widget refactors. WCAG AA contrast verified for every text-on-background combination in styling-design-pass.md §4.4.
after_payload.context: v2 at v0.4 ship has exactly one deliberately-chosen chroma color: the navy #1F3864 stub, used in two visible-to-user places — the sidebar stale-indicator dot (via QPainter on a QPixmap) and the error-dialog banner title text. Navy was never deliberately chosen as a brand accent; it was the closest at-hand 'make this feel official' pick during v0.1/v0.2 build. The styling Conversation 1 needed to settle (a) whether to ratify navy or pick a deliberate accent, (b) which color family to commit to, (c) whether to harmonize neutrals with the accent. Plus a 9-step neutral gray scale to replace the four ad-hoc grays currently in code (#f4f4f4 for read-only field backgrounds, #444 for read-only text and one of the dialog text variants, #666 for the dialog read-only text variant, #888 for secondary labels and captions).
after_payload.decision: **Cool-blue family.** Specifically color.accent.default = #1F5FBF (a moderately-saturated, slightly-lighter blue than the legacy navy stub) with derived variants: hover #2A6CCE, pressed #184F9F, subtle #E8F0FB (used for tinted-background treatment on selected sidebar/list rows), focusring color.accent.default at 40% alpha (used for keyboard-focus outline rings). Paired with a 9-step cool-gray neutral scale: color.neutral.0 #FFFFFF (modal backgrounds, contrast surfaces), neutral.50 #F7F9FB (primary panel surface — the dominant 'page' color), neutral.100 #EEF1F5 (subtle surface — alternating rows, sidebar background, read-only field background — retires the legacy #f4f4f4), neutral.200 #DDE3EA (hairline borders, table cell dividers), neutral.300 #C1CAD4 (disabled control fill, placeholder text), neutral.500 #7A8694 (secondary text and captions — retires the legacy #888), neutral.700 #3F4854 (read-only field text — retires the legacy #444 and #666), neutral.800 #272D36 (default body text), neutral.900 #0F1318 (high-emphasis text, headings). Drafted status colors harmonized with the cool blue: color.danger.default #C0392B (destructive button fill — retires legacy #c1272d), color.danger.text #A8281C (inline error/warning text — retires legacy #B22222), color.danger.subtle #FBEBE8, color.warning.default #B0731A (amber), color.warning.subtle #FBF2E0, color.success.default #2D7A4D (rare in v2).
after_payload.rationale: Four options weighed: keep #1F3864 navy (ratifies a default-by-accident color), pick a different blue (preserves family identity but signals deliberate choice), pick a non-blue accent (deep green / warm purple / muted plum — more distinctive but breaks visual continuity with existing v0.4 screenshots), grayscale-only with no chroma accent (too restrained for clear selection signals in a tool with frequent selection states). Different blue won. Follow-up sub-pick between brighter Tailwind-like #2563EB and steelier #1F5FBF: chose the steelier value on the reasoning that it matches a 'serious tool, deliberate choice' read better than the Tailwind-bright value, which leans consumer-SaaS-marketing-site. Cool grays follow naturally for pairing with a cool blue accent — warm grays would create slight visual tension, neutral grays read coherent but less unified. The 9-step scale was drafted in conversation as a set of values mapping to the recurring uses in v2's existing code, with deliberate retirement of the four ad-hoc gray values that had accumulated organically. Status colors were drafted to harmonize with the cool blue accent (slightly less orange-shifted than typical alert reds; amber rather than pure orange; muted green) and replace the legacy red and warning treatments in dialog and panel code.
after_payload.title: Brand accent #1F5FBF (steely blue) plus 9-step cool-gray neutral family

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Flat tokens (`color.accent.default = '#1F5FBF'`) — pure YAGNI. Rejected because the cost of the alternative structure today is negligible (~5 LOC) and the retrofit cost if a theme variant ships is non-trivial (rename every color token in code, refactor every consumer to be theme-aware).
after_payload.consequences: The tokens module (forthcoming in the build phase, expected at crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/ui/tokens.py) exposes a theme-keyed dict. A small accessor function or method serves as the consumer interface. Adding a future dark-mode theme requires authoring a `'dark'` dict with parallel values and exposing a theme-toggle mechanism — but does not require renaming any tokens or refactoring any consumer code. Future palette variants (CBM-branded, print-friendly, etc.) follow the same additive-only pattern.
after_payload.context: Color tokens can be structured as flat values (`color.accent.default = '#1F5FBF'`) or as theme-keyed dicts (`TOKENS['light']['color.accent.default'] = '#1F5FBF'`). The workstream plan §3.2 puts dark mode out of scope as an implementation, deferred to a separate PI if anyone asks for it. The styling Conversation 1 kickoff flagged dark-mode anticipation at the token-naming layer as a real fork. The remaining question to settle was the structural choice: whether to pay a small one-time cost to structure tokens such that adding dark mode later requires only adding values, or to keep tokens flat and accept the retrofit cost if dark mode is ever needed.
after_payload.decision: **Theme-keyed dict structure.** Tokens live in a dict keyed by theme name; the single theme today is `'light'`. Consumers access tokens through a small accessor function (`tokens.get('color.accent.default')` or similar) that reads `TOKENS[current_theme][key]` internally. Adding dark mode would be adding a `'dark'` sibling dict with parallel values; consumers don't change. No dark-mode values are authored in styling-design-pass.md today. The structure does not preclude them.
after_payload.rationale: We're building the tokens module from scratch in the build phase. Adding the theme-key dict layer costs one extra dict key today and one accessor function (~5 LOC). The alternative cost — flat tokens today, then retrofit if dark mode ever ships — is renaming every color token in code plus refactoring every consumer to be theme-aware. The structural choice is essentially free insurance against the future PI. The theme-keyed structure also pays an immediate non-dark-mode dividend: it cleanly separates 'what the values are' (data) from 'how they're consumed' (interface), which makes future palette experiments trivially additive — a CBM-branded palette variant, a print-friendly variant, etc., become 'add a theme dict' rather than 'fork and rename the consumer code.' This decision was handled as decide-and-announce rather than full eight-element pause because the cost differential today is negligible and the outcome differential only matters in worlds where dark mode (or a palette variant) eventually ships.
after_payload.title: Theme-keyed token-naming structure, light theme only today but dark-mode-ready without retrofit

after_payload.alternatives_considered: System-default fonts (status quo) — rejected for cross-platform divergence. Other open sans serifs considered briefly: Geist (Vercel's, OFL — 'Vercel deploy panel' aesthetic), IBM Plex Sans (IBM's, OFL — 'IBM enterprise' aesthetic), Source Sans 3 (Adobe's, OFL — 'Adobe creative tool' aesthetic), JetBrains Sans (newly open-sourced — 'IDE-ish' aesthetic). Each carries different aesthetic baggage; Inter was preferred as both ubiquitous and aesthetically neutral. Distinctive/mannered fonts considered briefly: Berkeley Mono (monospace-as-default — IDE/terminal aesthetic), Public Sans (US Web Design System — 'civic tech'), Atkinson Hyperlegible (Braille Institute — low-vision-optimized). Rejected as too mannered for v2's intended user-tool aesthetic. Alternative monospaces (Fira Code, IBM Plex Mono, Cascadia Code) were adequate but JetBrains Mono pairs aesthetically with the chosen design direction.
after_payload.consequences: Two font binaries committed to the repo at crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/ui/assets/fonts/ (or equivalent path settled in the build phase): Inter-Variable.ttf and JetBrainsMono-Variable.ttf. Two `QFontDatabase.addApplicationFont()` calls in the app startup path. `QApplication.setFont(QFont('Inter'))` sets the default. Per-widget overrides for monospace contexts via `setFont(QFont('JetBrains Mono'))` on `QLineEdit`s for identifier columns and on the About dialog's path-value labels. Cross-platform README screenshot consistency. Size, weight, and line-height scales are drafted in the design pass; refineable at build time without re-opening Conversation 1.
after_payload.context: v2 today renders with `QApplication.font()` default, which resolves to whatever the host OS provides — SF Pro Text on macOS Sonoma+, Segoe UI Variable on Windows 11, Ubuntu Sans / Liberation Sans on Linux. The mismatch between platforms makes README screenshots feel 'off' when viewed on a different platform, and design tokens for line-height and letter-spacing tuned for one font don't quite hit on the others. The styling Conversation 1 needed to settle font family choice at both the sans-serif (primary UI font) and monospace (identifier columns, code surfaces, About dialog paths) layers.
after_payload.decision: **Inter Variable as the sans-serif throughout the application; JetBrains Mono Variable as the monospace.** Both bundled in the repo as binary font files and loaded at app startup via `QFontDatabase.addApplicationFont()`. Inter set as the default via `QApplication.setFont(QFont('Inter'))`. Both fonts are OFL-licensed (free, redistributable). Total binary cost ~530 KB (~280 KB Inter + ~250 KB JetBrains Mono). Drafted size scale (font.size tokens): caption 12px (audit timestamps, sub-secondary text) / small 13px (master-pane row text, secondary form-field labels) / body 14px (default body, form fields, dialog text) / body_large 16px (detail-pane emphasized values) / heading_3 18px (detail-pane section headers, dialog section labels) / heading_2 22px (dialog title bars) / heading_1 28px (reserved). Drafted weight scale on Inter Variable's weight axis: regular 400 / medium 500 / semibold 600 / bold 700. Drafted line-height scale: tight 1.2 (headings) / normal 1.45 (body) / relaxed 1.6 (long-form prose — rare in v2). See styling-design-pass.md §1.3.
after_payload.rationale: Inter is the modern-dev-tool default for cross-platform consistency — used by Linear, Figma, Stripe internal tools, GitHub Primer, shadcn/ui, and effectively the convention for any new web/desktop product targeting a 'designed' aesthetic. Bundling costs are small: ~280 KB binary, one font-load call at app startup. Inter's variable-font format provides the full weight axis (400 through 700) without bundling separate font files per weight. The retrofit cost if v2 ships with system-default fonts and switches to bundled later is also low (a one-line change in app startup), but consistency in README screenshots and overall coherence improves immediately with bundling. JetBrains Mono adds at the monospace layer for identifier columns (DEC-077 and similar align cleanly by their numeric suffix) and for the About dialog's path values where the current code already implicitly assumes a monospace font but doesn't enforce one.
after_payload.title: Font stack: Inter Variable + JetBrains Mono Variable, both bundled at app startup

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Fully flat throughout (Option A) — no shadows, no overlay backdrop. Rejected because flat-aesthetic modals can fail to register on Linux compositors with minimal window decoration. Broader elevation tiers (Option C) — modals + popup combos + selected list rows + focused buttons, multiple z-tiers. Rejected for platform-rendering inconsistency and the discipline cost of maintaining multiple elevation profiles.
after_payload.consequences: Every modal dialog's `__init__` adds a `QGraphicsDropShadowEffect` setup pattern. The backdrop overlay is a new small widget that paints over the main window content while a modal is active. The radius.dialog token (6px, drafted in §1.4) pairs with the soft shadow on platforms that render rounded dialog corners. No QSS box-shadow rules anywhere — shadows come exclusively from the QGraphicsDropShadowEffect API. The build phase will need to verify that the shadow renders acceptably on macOS, Ubuntu (Yaru / Gnome / KDE), and that the backdrop fade-in feels smooth. Implementation complexity: low; a small helper function `apply_dialog_elevation(dialog)` plus the backdrop widget.
after_payload.context: Today modals open directly over the underlying panel with no visual separation other than the OS-drawn window frame. The dialog sits in the same z-plane visually; the eye has to use the title bar and window outline to register 'this is a modal.' Flat-aesthetic v2 risks modal-disambiguation failure on some Linux compositors where window decorations vary. The styling Conversation 1 needed to settle whether v2 has any visual depth at all and, if so, where it applies. QSS box-shadow doesn't render reliably in PySide6, so any drop-shadow approach uses either Qt's built-in QGraphicsDropShadowEffect (simple, performs adequately, applies to any QWidget) or custom paintEvent overrides (heavyweight, platform-fragile). Workstream plan §3.2 explicitly puts custom paintEvent out of scope; QGraphicsDropShadowEffect remains within the QSS-supplemented-by-built-in-effects posture.
after_payload.decision: **Elevation only on modal dialogs.** Every `QDialog` subclass — CRUD dialogs, references attach/delete, version-replace, About, error dialog — gets a `QGraphicsDropShadowEffect` applied in `__init__`. Single elevation token `shadow.dialog` applied uniformly: Y-offset 4px, X-offset 0px, blur radius 16px, color color.neutral.900 at 25% alpha. Plus `overlay.modal_backdrop` — a full-window overlay widget at color.neutral.900 8% alpha that fades in over ~150ms when a modal opens, dimming the underlying panel. Rest of the app — sidebar, panels, master pane, detail pane, fields, buttons — shares one z-plane and stays fully flat. No popup-shadow tier, no focused-button micro-elevation, no selected-row shadow.
after_payload.rationale: Modal disambiguation is the single highest-value depth cue in an otherwise flat application. One Qt effect setup per dialog (3-5 lines) plus one design token plus one backdrop widget resolves the most common flat-design failure mode (modal-vs-underlying-panel confusion, especially on Linux compositors with variable window decorations) without committing to multi-tier elevation discipline anywhere else. Going broader — popup shadows on combo dropdowns, focused-button micro-elevation, selected-row shadows — would buy aesthetic polish at the cost of platform-rendering inconsistency. Popup shadows in particular render inconsistently across Qt platforms and clip oddly under some Linux compositors. The single-tier approach is the right level of investment for v0.5/v0.6 timeframe.
after_payload.title: Modal elevation only — single shadow.dialog token via QGraphicsDropShadowEffect; rest of app flat

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Qt-built-in / system-theme icons via QIcon::fromTheme (Option A) — rejected for cross-platform inconsistency (works on Linux, empty on macOS/Windows). Phosphor (Option B) — rejected for aesthetic mismatch with the restrained design direction (warmer rounded weight reads 'modern product' rather than 'deliberate tool').
after_payload.consequences: Initial set of ~10-15 SVGs committed to the repo. Small icon-loading helper in ui/icons.py (or similar) exposes a `lucide(name, size=16, color_token=...)` interface returning `QIcon`. Per-icon color application happens at runtime via the design token system — the same SVG asset renders in color.neutral.700 for a default button and color.neutral.0 for a primary button. Icon set grows additively as components need new icons during the build phase; no separate process needed to request additions. Build phase verifies the QSvgRenderer-to-QIcon conversion handles HiDPI correctly across the three target platforms.
after_payload.context: v2 today is effectively icon-free — context menus render text-only (Edit / Delete / Restore / Go to references / Copy identifier — all words, no icons), toolbars render text-only, dialog buttons render text-only, sidebar entries are text-only. As the styling pass settles component visual decisions (context menus, button affordances for 'Add reference,' status-indicator dots, possibly sidebar group icons in future), v2 needs a consistent icon source. Three options were considered: Qt-built-in / system-theme icons via `QIcon::fromTheme` (free, available, but inconsistent on Linux and largely empty on macOS/Windows without explicit fallback paths), Phosphor (~7,000 icons across 6 weight variants — thin/light/regular/bold/fill/duotone — MIT-licensed, warmer rounded aesthetic), Lucide (~1,400 icons, single regular stroke at 2px uniform width, ISC-licensed, minimal/restrained aesthetic, used by shadcn / Vercel / many modern dev tools).
after_payload.decision: **Lucide.** Icons bundled additively in the repo at crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/ui/assets/icons/lucide/{kebab-case-name}.svg as components need them — no speculative pre-bundle of all 1,400 icons. Small loader helper wraps `QSvgRenderer` to produce `QIcon` at requested size, with runtime color tinting via the design token system. Default icon size 16px (matches body text height closely; readable at 4K HiDPI). Initial wave of ~10-15 icons anticipated to cover the surfaces designed in Conversation 1: pencil (Edit), trash-2 (Delete), rotate-ccw (Restore), external-link (Go to references), copy (Copy identifier), plus (Add / New), x (Close / Dismiss), chevron-right / chevron-down (collapsible toggles, sort indicators), circle-alert (warning), circle-x (error), check (success affirmation, checkbox checked state), asterisk (required-field marker). Sketch of the loader interface: `def lucide(name: str, *, size: int = 16, color_token: str = 'color.neutral.700') -> QIcon: ...`.
after_payload.rationale: Lucide's minimal 2px-uniform-stroke aesthetic pairs cleanly with Inter and the restrained color direction (cool blue accent, cool grays, deliberate-but-quiet design language). Phosphor's warmer rounded weight reads slightly mismatched with this direction — Phosphor's aesthetic is 'modern designed product,' Lucide's is 'deliberate restrained tool,' and Lucide matches v2's positioning better. Qt-built-in icons via QIcon::fromTheme work inconsistently on Linux (depend on the installed icon theme — Yaru / Adwaita / Breeze all differ) and return empty on macOS/Windows without explicit fallback paths — effectively text-only-on-most-platforms in practice. Lucide's single-weight constraint means fewer choices to discipline during build (no per-context weight selection); every icon renders at the same visual weight. Total binary cost is small — Lucide icons are tiny (~1-2 KB each) and the initial bundle is ~10-15 icons (~20 KB total).
after_payload.title: Lucide icon library, bundled additively as components need icons

after_payload.alternatives_considered: Tinted background only (Option C in sidebar conversation) — rejected because hover risks blurring with selected unless hover is designed to be meaningfully different. Full-fill with accent (Option B) — rejected as heavy in long lists. Right-edge accent bar (Option D, GitHub-style) — rejected as less conventional than left-bar; would mismatch user expectations from VS Code / JetBrains / Linear / shadcn. Border outline (Option E) — rejected as reading more like a button than a selected row. Different selection vocabularies for sidebar versus master pane — rejected; system coherence wins over per-surface optimization. For master pane banding: alternating row shading (Option B) — rejected as appropriate for high-density spreadsheet contexts, overweight for v2's 22-row-visible panes. Pure minimal with no dividers (Option A) — rejected as feeling under-organized for a data-table surface.
after_payload.consequences: A shared `QStyledItemDelegate` subclass (forthcoming in the build phase) renders the left-bar + tint pattern across all 12 panels' master views (eventually 13 once v0.5 adds the engagement panel). The sidebar's QListWidget either inherits a similar delegate or gets paint-event overrides. Both implementations are called out in styling-design-pass.md §3.1 as high-attention surfaces for the build phase. The soft-deleted-row visual (when 'include deleted' is on) renders text in color.neutral.500 with a leading Lucide trash-2 icon at 14px in the identifier column; this also belongs in the shared delegate. Identifier columns across all master panes render in font.family.mono (JetBrains Mono) at font.size.small so identifiers like DEC-077 align cleanly by their numeric suffix. The keyboard-focused-state outline drawing on master-pane rows is constrained inside the cell area, not crossing the row divider — a subtle build-phase implementation detail.
after_payload.context: The sidebar and the master pane (the per-panel `QTableView` / `QTreeView` showing records) are the two surfaces in v2 where users see 'selected' most often. Today both rely on OS-default selection treatment — system blue rectangle on macOS Sonoma+, orange-tinted on Ubuntu Yaru, etc. — inconsistent across platforms and undifferentiated from each other. The styling Conversation 1 needed to settle the visual vocabulary for selected state in both surfaces. Coupled decision because using different vocabularies in the two places would force users to learn two patterns; using the same vocabulary ties the design system together. Several visual options were on the table: tinted background only (restrained but risks blurring with hover), full-fill with accent color and white text (heavy in a long list), left-edge accent bar plus tinted background (modern dev-tool convention — VS Code, JetBrains, Linear, shadcn), right-edge accent bar (GitHub-left-nav style), border outline (reads more like a button). For the master pane specifically, additional sub-question of whether to use alternating row shading (zebra striping) for visual structure.
after_payload.decision: **Left accent bar plus tinted background, applied to both sidebar entries and master-pane rows.** 3px-wide vertical bar in color.accent.default (#1F5FBF) flush against the row/entry's left edge, plus color.accent.subtle (#E8F0FB) tinted background, plus text bump to color.neutral.900 at font.weight.medium for slight emphasis. The master pane requires a custom QStyledItemDelegate subclass to render the left bar (Qt's default item rendering does not support this pattern through QSS); the sidebar similarly needs a delegate or paint-event override on QListWidget. Master pane specifics: row dividers (1px hairline color.neutral.200 below each row) but no alternating row shading; column header background color.neutral.100 with bottom hairline color.neutral.200 and font.weight.semibold color.neutral.700 text. Sidebar specifics: distinct hover treatment (color.neutral.200 background, no left bar) so hover and selected never blur. Keyboard-focused state on both surfaces reuses the selected visuals plus a 1px color.accent.focusring outline drawn outside the bounds. See styling-design-pass.md §§2.1 and 2.3.
after_payload.rationale: The left accent bar plus tinted background is the modern dev-tool convention for good reason: it gives selection a strong, peripheral-vision-readable signal without dominating the visual field. Full-fill with accent (alternative B in conversation) reads heavy in a 12+ item sidebar — the eye repeatedly snaps to whichever single item is selected. Tinted-background-only (alternative C) risks blurring with hover unless hover is meaningfully different (which then requires designing two separate treatments without a clear hierarchy). Border outline reads more like a button than a selected row. Right-edge bar is GitHub-style but less ubiquitous; left-bar is the broadly-recognized convention. Reusing the same vocabulary across sidebar and master pane keeps the design system tight: users learn 'selected = left bar + tint' once, and the rule applies wherever they encounter it.
